parts.notes: The cap contains the highest concentration of psychoactive compounds. Traditional preparation involves thorough drying to convert neurotoxic ibotenic acid to the desired muscimol.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Sedative, analgesic
parts.notes: An extract or tea can be prepared from dried specimens, allowing more precise dosing than consuming whole mushroom material.

planet_associations.notes: Neptune governs the dreamy, trance-like, and boundary-dissolving delirium characteristic of the muscimol experience.
Jupiter reflects the mythic, expansive, and culturally archetypal nature of fly agaric, which has permeated folklore, religion, and fairy tales across the Northern Hemisphere.

zodiac_associations.notes: Pisces mirrors the dreamy, trance-like dissolution of ordinary reality and surrender to the unconscious currents that define the fly agaric experience.
Sagittarius resonates with the mythic, archetypal, and culturally expansive role of fly agaric across shamanic traditions worldwide.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Siberian shamans have used fly agaric for millennia to enter trance states for healing, divination, and spirit communication.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-3 thoroughly dried caps prepared as tea; potency varies enormously and caution is paramount.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Muscimol's GABAergic action produces deep sedation that has been explored for sleep disorders in microdose protocols.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Microdoses of dried cap material (0.5-1g) as tea before sleep; highly experimental.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Traditional use as a topical and internal analgesic in Russian and Scandinavian folk medicine for musculoskeletal pain.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Topical tincture applied to painful joints; internal use requires extreme caution with precise dosing.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Muscimol's GABAergic activity may modulate neuropathic pain signaling; traditional topical applications for nerve pain are well documented.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Topical tincture of dried cap material applied to affected areas.

contraindication_associations.reason: Contains ibotenic acid and muscimol which act on GABA and glutamate receptors; fetal neurotoxicity risk from excitotoxic and GABAergic mechanisms
contraindication_associations.notes: Toxic mushroom alkaloids pose serious risk to fetal neurological development; avoid completely

contraindication_associations.reason: Muscimol is a potent GABA-A receptor agonist and ibotenic acid is a glutamate receptor agonist; this combination produces unpredictable psychoactive effects including delirium and agitation
contraindication_associations.notes: Effects are unpredictable and dose-dependent; high risk of severe confusion, agitation, and psychotic symptoms

contraindication_associations.reason: Ibotenic acid is a known excitotoxin and NMDA receptor agonist that can directly provoke seizure activity
contraindication_associations.notes: Ibotenic acid has documented proconvulsant properties; absolutely avoid with seizure history

contraindication_associations.reason: Contains multiple toxic alkaloids requiring hepatic detoxification; impaired liver function increases accumulation of ibotenic acid and muscimol
contraindication_associations.notes: Hepatotoxic potential increases with impaired clearance; liver damage cases reported with Amanita species

contraindication_associations.reason: Muscimol and ibotenic acid are primarily excreted renally; impaired renal function prolongs exposure to neurotoxic compounds
contraindication_associations.notes: Renal impairment may significantly extend duration of toxic effects

description: A hardy desert plant whose seeds contain potent MAO-inhibiting harmala alkaloids, Syrian rue has served as a protective fumigant, sacred incense, and visionary catalyst across the Middle East and Central Asia for millennia. It is the suspected identity of the ancient Persian sacrament haoma.
habitat: Arid and semi-arid steppes, roadsides, and disturbed ground, thriving in poor, alkaline soils with minimal water.
native_region: Eastern Mediterranean, Middle East, Central Asia, and North Africa
energetic_quality: Warming and drying, with a penetrating, purifying quality that clears stagnation and opens channels of perception.
doctrine_of_signatures: The plant's ability to thrive in harsh, barren landscapes mirrors its capacity to bring vision and vitality to psychic wastelands. Its seeds, when burned, produce a dense protective smoke, reflecting their role as spiritual cleansers and boundary-keepers.
safety_notes: Contains potent MAO inhibitors; dangerous or fatal interactions with tyramine-rich foods, SSRIs, stimulants, and many medications. Must observe strict dietary restrictions. Causes nausea and tremors. Contraindicated in pregnancy, liver disease, and cardiac conditions. Never combine with serotonergic substances.

parts.therapeutic_properties: MAO inhibitor, visionary, antiparasitic, fumigant, antidepressant
parts.notes: Seeds are the primary medicinal and entheogenic material, used as incense, extracted as tea, or ground for ingestion. Critical to observe MAOI dietary and pharmaceutical restrictions.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Antiparasitic, uterotonic, antimicrobial
parts.notes: Roots contain harmala alkaloids and vasicine, traditionally used in Middle Eastern and Central Asian folk medicine for parasitic and reproductive concerns.

planet_associations.notes: Mars governs Syrian rue's fierce protective quality, its burning purification, and its capacity to cut through spiritual and physical stagnation.
Neptune reflects the visionary and consciousness-expanding properties activated when harmala alkaloids potentiate other entheogens.

zodiac_associations.notes: Aries mirrors Syrian rue's fiery, protective, and boundary-establishing nature—the spiritual warrior's fumigant and shield.
Scorpio resonates with the plant's association with purification, transformation, and the uncovering of hidden forces through its MAO-inhibiting revelation.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Harmine and harmaline have demonstrated significant antiparasitic activity against multiple parasitic organisms in laboratory and traditional settings.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 3-5g seeds decocted; short-term use only with strict MAOI dietary restrictions observed.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: The reversible MAO-A inhibition of harmine produces antidepressant effects comparable to pharmaceutical MAOIs.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Low doses of 1-3g seed tea; strict dietary and pharmaceutical restrictions must be observed.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Harmala alkaloids alone can produce visionary states at higher doses and are the suspected basis of the ancient Zoroastrian sacrament haoma.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 3-5g seeds as tea for standalone visionary effects; lower doses used as potentiator.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: The smoke of burning Syrian rue seeds has been used for millennia across the Middle East and Central Asia as a protective and spiritually cleansing fumigant.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Seeds burned as incense in ritual context; this external use does not require MAOI dietary precautions.

contraindication_associations.reason: Contains harmine and harmaline which are beta-carboline alkaloids with uterotonic properties; traditionally used as an abortifacient
contraindication_associations.notes: Historically documented abortifacient; causes uterine contractions and poses high miscarriage risk

contraindication_associations.reason: Harmine and harmaline are potent reversible MAO-A inhibitors that dramatically increase serotonin, norepinephrine, and dopamine levels, risking serotonin syndrome
contraindication_associations.notes: Extremely dangerous with SSRIs, SNRIs, tricyclics, or any serotonergic medication; risk of fatal serotonin syndrome

contraindication_associations.reason: MAO inhibition causes unpredictable hypertensive responses, especially with tyramine-containing foods; beta-carboline alkaloids also have direct cardiac effects
contraindication_associations.notes: Risk of hypertensive crisis; strict dietary tyramine restrictions required; avoid with any cardiac condition

contraindication_associations.reason: MAO-A inhibition prevents breakdown of norepinephrine and tyramine, causing potentially fatal hypertensive crises
contraindication_associations.notes: Extremely dangerous in hypertensive patients; tyramine interaction can produce catastrophic blood pressure spikes

contraindication_associations.reason: Beta-carboline alkaloids undergo hepatic metabolism; harmine and harmaline are hepatotoxic at elevated concentrations and impaired clearance increases toxicity
contraindication_associations.notes: Liver function must be adequate for safe metabolism of MAO-inhibiting alkaloids

contraindication_associations.reason: Beta-carboline alkaloids modulate GABAergic neurotransmission and elevated monoamine levels from MAO inhibition can lower seizure threshold
contraindication_associations.notes: May interact with anticonvulsant medications; harmaline has tremorigenic properties at higher doses

description: A towering Southeast Asian tree whose leaves contain a complex symphony of alkaloids that dance between stimulation and sedation depending on dose. Kratom is the working person's medicine, long used by Thai and Malaysian laborers, and now emerging as a controversial ally in the opioid crisis.
habitat: Tropical lowland forests and riverbanks in hot, humid climates with rich, waterlogged soils and consistent rainfall.
native_region: Thailand, Malaysia, Indonesia, Myanmar, Papua New Guinea
energetic_quality: Dose-dependent: warming and stimulating at low doses, sedating and analgesic at higher doses. A dual-natured plant that meets the user where they are.
doctrine_of_signatures: The large, glossy leaves of this towering tree suggest abundance and generosity. The tree's affinity for riverbanks—the boundary between land and water—mirrors kratom's position at the threshold between stimulant and sedative, between legal and prohibited.
safety_notes: Risk of dependence and withdrawal with regular use. Contraindicated with opioids, benzodiazepines, alcohol, and other CNS depressants. Liver toxicity reported in rare cases. Quality and contamination concerns with unregulated products. Legal status varies widely by jurisdiction.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Analgesic, stimulant, sedative (dose-dependent), anxiolytic, anti-addictive
parts.notes: Leaves are the sole medicinal part, traditionally chewed fresh or brewed as tea. Vein color (red, green, white) correlates with different alkaloid profiles and effects.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Astringent, mild analgesic
parts.notes: Bark contains lower alkaloid concentrations and is occasionally used in traditional preparations but is not the primary medicinal material.

planet_associations.notes: Jupiter reflects kratom's generous, abundant nature, its large tropical tree form, and its capacity to provide relief and uplift across a broad spectrum of human suffering.
Mars resonates with kratom's stimulant properties at low doses, its use by laborers for endurance, and its fiercely contested legal and cultural status.

zodiac_associations.notes: Sagittarius mirrors kratom's Jupiterian generosity, its Southeast Asian origins suggesting expansive cultural reach, and its dual nature bridging opposites.
Scorpio resonates with kratom's opioid-receptor activity, its complex relationship with addiction, and its transformative potential in recovery contexts.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Mitragynine and 7-hydroxymitragynine act on mu-opioid receptors to provide significant pain relief without full opioid respiratory depression.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Analgesic doses typically 3-7g dried leaf as tea; lower doses (1-3g) are stimulating rather than analgesic.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Widely used for self-management of opioid withdrawal symptoms; partial agonist activity may ease the transition away from full opioid agonists.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 3-8g dried leaf as tea, titrated to manage withdrawal symptoms; taper strategy recommended for long-term use.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: At low doses, mitragynine acts as a stimulant comparable to its relative coffee, providing sustained energy without the jitteriness of caffeine.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-3g dried leaf as tea for stimulant effects; traditionally chewed fresh by laborers throughout the workday.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Moderate doses produce anxiolytic effects through combined opioid and adrenergic receptor activity.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 2-5g dried leaf as tea; effects are dose-sensitive and individual response varies significantly.

contraindication_associations.reason: Mitragynine and 7-hydroxymitragynine are opioid receptor agonists that cross the placenta; neonatal abstinence syndrome documented in infants born to kratom-using mothers
contraindication_associations.notes: Neonatal withdrawal syndrome reported; opioid effects on fetal development include respiratory depression risk

contraindication_associations.reason: Multiple case reports of kratom-induced hepatotoxicity; mitragynine undergoes extensive CYP3A4 and CYP2D6 metabolism with hepatotoxic metabolites identified
contraindication_associations.notes: Documented cases of cholestatic liver injury and acute hepatitis from kratom use; monitor liver enzymes

contraindication_associations.reason: Kratom alkaloids lower the seizure threshold; seizures reported in both acute use and withdrawal, particularly with high-dose or chronic use
contraindication_associations.notes: Seizures documented in clinical case reports; risk increases with dose escalation and concurrent medications

contraindication_associations.reason: Opioidergic and adrenergic activity can exacerbate depression, anxiety, and psychosis; chronic use leads to physical dependence with psychiatric withdrawal symptoms
contraindication_associations.notes: Dependence potential is significant; withdrawal includes anxiety, depression, irritability, and insomnia

contraindication_associations.reason: Kratom has been associated with tachycardia, hypertension, and rare cases of cardiac arrest; mitragynine may affect cardiac ion channels
contraindication_associations.notes: Cardiac toxicity reports exist though mechanism not fully elucidated; avoid with arrhythmia history

contraindication_associations.reason: Chronic kratom use associated with nephrotoxicity; alkaloid metabolites are renally excreted and may accumulate with impaired renal function
contraindication_associations.notes: Cases of acute kidney injury reported; adequate renal function important for safe clearance

description: The intoxicating pepper of the Pacific Islands, kava is the social sacrament and peacemaking medicine of Oceanic culture, turning strangers into kin through the shared bowl. Its roots dissolve anxiety and social tension while preserving mental clarity—a rare and precious combination.
habitat: Tropical Pacific island environments with rich volcanic soil, high humidity, dappled shade, and consistent rainfall.
native_region: Vanuatu, Fiji, Tonga, Samoa, Hawaii, and other Pacific Islands
energetic_quality: Cooling, relaxing, and grounding, with a numbing quality that begins on the tongue and spreads through the body as a deep muscular and emotional release.
doctrine_of_signatures: The knotted, gnarled roots suggest the tangled knots of tension and anxiety that kava unties. The plant's requirement for shade mirrors its ability to provide shelter and comfort from the harsh light of social stress and overstimulation.
safety_notes: Hepatotoxicity concerns primarily associated with non-root parts and solvent extracts; traditional aqueous preparations of noble cultivars have excellent safety records. Contraindicated with alcohol, benzodiazepines, and hepatotoxic medications. May cause dermopathy with heavy chronic use.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Anxiolytic, muscle relaxant, analgesic, anticonvulsant, euphoric
parts.notes: The lateral root and rootstock are the traditional medicine, prepared as a cold-water infusion. Noble cultivar roots are preferred for safety and desirable effects.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Anxiolytic, potentially hepatotoxic
parts.notes: Stem peelings (called lewena in Fiji) contain flavokavains associated with liver toxicity and are excluded from traditional noble kava preparations.

planet_associations.notes: The Moon governs kava's soothing, nurturing quality, its connection to communal bonding, emotional comfort, and the twilight ceremonies of Pacific island culture.
Venus reflects kava's role in social harmony, peacemaking, and the pleasure of communal connection through the shared bowl.

zodiac_associations.notes: Cancer embodies kava's nurturing, emotionally soothing, and community-building qualities, as well as its connection to home, tradition, and ancestral custom.
Libra resonates with kava's role in diplomacy, social harmony, and the balancing of tensions within community.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Multiple clinical trials demonstrate kavalactones are as effective as benzodiazepines for generalized anxiety without cognitive impairment or dependence.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Traditional preparation: 30-50g root kneaded in cold water. Standardized extracts: 100-250mg kavalactones daily.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Kava improves sleep quality and reduces sleep onset latency through GABAergic and sodium channel activity without suppressing REM sleep.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Traditional evening preparation of 30-50g root; heavier cultivars preferred for sleep.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: The unique combination of anxiolysis with preserved mental clarity makes kava exceptionally suited for stress management without functional impairment.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Moderate traditional preparation; daily use acceptable with noble cultivars and periodic breaks.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Kavalactones provide analgesic and muscle-relaxant effects useful for tension-related and musculoskeletal pain.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Traditional preparation focused on heavier cultivars; topical application of masticated root also traditional for localized pain.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Kavalactones provide anxiolytic effects comparable to benzodiazepines for generalized anxiety, without cognitive impairment.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 120-240 mg kavalactones daily. Monitor liver function.

contraindication_associations.reason: Kavalactones cross the placental barrier and may affect fetal muscle tone through GABA modulation; uterine relaxant effects may complicate labor
contraindication_associations.notes: Insufficient safety data for pregnancy; traditional Pacific Island cultures restrict kava use during pregnancy

contraindication_associations.reason: Well-documented hepatotoxicity risk; kavalactones and their metabolites inhibit CYP450 enzymes and cause idiosyncratic liver injury including fulminant hepatic failure
contraindication_associations.notes: Multiple countries have restricted or banned kava due to hepatotoxicity reports; cases of liver transplant and death documented

contraindication_associations.reason: Kavalactones potentiate GABAergic sedation and may interact dangerously with benzodiazepines, antidepressants, and antipsychotics
contraindication_associations.notes: Additive CNS depression with psychiatric medications; may mask symptoms requiring clinical attention

contraindication_associations.reason: Kavalactones are lipophilic compounds likely excreted in breast milk; GABAergic effects may cause sedation in nursing infants
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid during breastfeeding due to potential infant sedation and hepatotoxicity risk

description: The thornapple, devil's snare, jimsonweed—datura is the most fearsome and demanding of the solanaceous teacher plants, inducing true delirium and communion with the shadow realm. It is the plant of sorcerers and boundary-walkers, demanding absolute respect and offering no forgiveness for carelessness.
habitat: Disturbed ground, roadsides, vacant lots, and agricultural margins worldwide, thriving in nitrogen-rich soils and full sun.
native_region: Uncertain; possibly Central America or Central Asia, now distributed globally
energetic_quality: Extremely hot and drying, with an aggressive, boundary-dissolving quality that overrides the rational mind and plunges consciousness into the underworld of hallucination.
doctrine_of_signatures: The spiny seed pods resemble medieval weapons, warning of danger. The trumpet-shaped flowers open at dusk, signaling datura's affinity for the liminal hours between worlds. The entire plant has a fetid smell that repels—nature's clear boundary marker.
safety_notes: EXTREMELY DANGEROUS. Alkaloid content varies wildly between plants, plant parts, and even leaves on the same plant, making safe dosing nearly impossible. Overdose causes hyperthermia, seizures, coma, and death. Produces true delirium with complete inability to distinguish hallucination from reality. Many fatalities recorded. Not recommended for ingestion under any circumstances.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Deliriant, antispasmodic, bronchodilator, analgesic
parts.notes: Leaves were historically smoked for asthma relief and used in surgical anesthesia. Alkaloid content is highly variable and unpredictable.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Deliriant, entheogenic, anticholinergic, analgesic
parts.notes: Seeds contain the highest and most variable alkaloid concentrations. The spiny seed pods contain dozens to hundreds of small black seeds.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Analgesic, sedative, anticholinergic
parts.notes: Roots were historically used in topical preparations and ritual ointments. Lower alkaloid variability than seeds but still dangerous.

planet_associations.notes: Saturn governs datura's association with death, restriction, madness, and the darkest boundaries of human experience.
Pluto reflects datura's capacity to drag consciousness into the underworld, its association with sorcery, and its demand for complete ego death.

zodiac_associations.notes: Scorpio rules the death-and-rebirth territory, the shadow realm, and the underworld encounters that define the datura experience.
Capricorn reflects datura's Saturnian harshness, its demand for absolute respect of boundaries, and the severe consequences of carelessness.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Historically used as an antispasmodic bronchodilator for asthma; atropine relaxes bronchial smooth muscle.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Dried leaf was smoked in small quantities for asthma historically; now replaced by safer pharmaceutical alternatives.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Topical applications of datura preparations have a long history for musculoskeletal and neuralgic pain relief.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: External use only as topical liniment; transdermal absorption still presents anticholinergic risks.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Used by indigenous peoples of the Americas in initiatory rites and sorcerous practice, though the experience is delirious rather than lucid.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Traditional use only under experienced guidance; no safe dose can be recommended due to extreme alkaloid variability.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Scopolamine's sedative properties were historically employed for intractable insomnia, though modern use is not recommended.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Historical use only; safer alternatives exist. Scopolamine is now available in pharmaceutical transdermal patches for specific medical indications.

contraindication_associations.reason: Tropane alkaloids (atropine, scopolamine, hyoscyamine) cross the placenta and can cause fetal tachycardia, anticholinergic toxicity, and neonatal withdrawal
contraindication_associations.notes: Tropane alkaloids are teratogenic; fetal anticholinergic syndrome and meconium ileus reported

contraindication_associations.reason: Atropine and scopolamine block muscarinic receptors causing severe tachycardia, arrhythmias, and potential cardiac arrest at toxic doses
contraindication_associations.notes: Anticholinergic tachycardia places extreme stress on compromised hearts; potentially fatal with existing cardiac conditions

contraindication_associations.reason: Anticholinergic delirium produces vivid hallucinations, severe agitation, and complete loss of reality contact; effects last 24-72 hours and can trigger lasting psychosis
contraindication_associations.notes: Considered one of the most dangerous deliriants; induces true psychotic delirium indistinguishable from acute psychosis

contraindication_associations.reason: Anticholinergic toxicity can cause convulsions through CNS excitation; tropane alkaloids lower seizure threshold at toxic doses
contraindication_associations.notes: Seizures are a recognized feature of severe anticholinergic poisoning; potentially fatal

contraindication_associations.reason: Anticholinergic alkaloids reduce gastric motility and may mask ulcer pain while inhibiting protective mucus secretion; impaired GI function increases complications
contraindication_associations.notes: Reduced gastric motility can worsen gastrointestinal conditions; delays gastric emptying significantly

contraindication_associations.reason: Anticholinergic effects cause urinary retention which can be dangerous with existing renal impairment; tropane alkaloids are partially renally cleared
contraindication_associations.notes: Urinary retention is a common anticholinergic effect; may precipitate acute renal crisis in compromised kidneys

description: The black henbane, herb of the oracles and witches' gardens, has served as a bridge to the spirit world across European and Middle Eastern traditions since antiquity. Its sticky, fetid foliage and skull-like seed pods mark it unmistakably as a plant of the threshold between life and death.
habitat: Waste ground, roadsides, old ruins, and disturbed soils across temperate regions, often growing near human habitation and in nitrogen-rich soils.
native_region: Europe, western Asia, and North Africa
energetic_quality: Cold and narcotic, with a heavy, dream-laden quality that pulls consciousness downward into somnolent trance and prophetic sleep.
doctrine_of_signatures: The seed capsules resemble rows of grinning skulls with open mouths, unmistakably marking henbane as a plant of death and the underworld. The sticky, clammy leaves and fetid odor repel the living while beckoning the shade-walker.
safety_notes: Highly toxic with unpredictable alkaloid concentrations. Causes delirium, tachycardia, hyperthermia, paralysis, and potentially death. Historically responsible for numerous accidental poisonings. No safe dose can be recommended for internal use. Historical use only under expert guidance.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Narcotic, analgesic, antispasmodic, sedative
parts.notes: Leaves were historically smoked or incorporated into ointments for pain relief and prophetic trance. Used in ancient Greek oracle traditions.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Narcotic, analgesic, deliriant, dental analgesic
parts.notes: Seeds were burned and the smoke inhaled for toothache relief in European folk medicine, a practice dating to ancient Egypt. Higher alkaloid content than leaves.

planet_associations.notes: Saturn governs henbane's cold, narcotic, death-associated qualities, its connection to boundaries, restriction, and the passage between worlds.
Neptune reflects henbane's capacity to dissolve the boundary between waking and dreaming, between the living world and the realm of shades.

zodiac_associations.notes: Capricorn embodies henbane's Saturnian coldness, its association with bone-deep endurance, and its historical connection to the darkest passages of initiation.
Scorpio resonates with henbane's underworld associations, its connection to death mysteries, and its role in necromantic and oracular traditions.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Historically one of the most valued analgesics in European medicine, used topically in ointments for rheumatic, neuralgic, and surgical pain.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: External use only as topical ointment; transdermal absorption requires caution.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: The fumigation of henbane seeds for toothache is one of the oldest recorded dental remedies, documented from ancient Egypt through medieval Europe.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Seeds placed on hot coals with fumes directed to the affected tooth; a topical application rather than systemic ingestion.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Scopolamine and hyoscyamine produce profound sedation; historically used for intractable insomnia and pre-surgical anesthesia.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Historical pharmaceutical preparations only; no modern self-dosing recommended.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Used in Greek and Near Eastern oracle traditions to induce prophetic trance and communication with the dead.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Traditional oracular use only; the line between visionary dose and toxic dose is dangerously narrow.

contraindication_associations.reason: Contains hyoscyamine, scopolamine, and atropine which cross the placenta; tropane alkaloids associated with fetal anticholinergic toxicity and tachycardia
contraindication_associations.notes: Tropane alkaloid content makes this an absolute contraindication during pregnancy

contraindication_associations.reason: Tropane alkaloids block cardiac muscarinic receptors causing dose-dependent tachycardia that can progress to dangerous arrhythmias and cardiac arrest
contraindication_associations.notes: Anticholinergic cardiac effects are unpredictable due to variable alkaloid content in plant material

contraindication_associations.reason: Scopolamine and hyoscyamine produce anticholinergic delirium with terrifying hallucinations, severe disorientation, and amnesia lasting days
contraindication_associations.notes: Delirium is not a psychedelic experience but a toxic psychosis; may trigger lasting psychiatric complications

contraindication_associations.reason: CNS excitation from anticholinergic toxicity can precipitate tonic-clonic seizures; risk is dose-dependent and unpredictable with crude plant material
contraindication_associations.notes: Convulsions are part of the anticholinergic toxidrome; absolute contraindication with seizure disorders

contraindication_associations.reason: Anticholinergic effects suppress gastric acid secretion but also reduce protective mucus production and impair GI motility, complicating ulcer management
contraindication_associations.notes: May mask ulcer symptoms while worsening underlying pathology; gastroparesis risk

contraindication_associations.reason: Urinary retention from anticholinergic activity can precipitate acute renal complications; impaired renal clearance prolongs toxic effects
contraindication_associations.notes: Urinary retention is dose-dependent and potentially severe; dangerous with pre-existing renal insufficiency

description: Named for Atropos, the Fate who cuts the thread of life, belladonna is the queen of the poison garden and the most storied of European witchcraft plants. Its gleaming black berries are the forbidden fruit of the hedge-witch's pharmacopoeia, beautiful, deadly, and deeply initiatory.
habitat: Shaded woodland margins, ruins, and limestone-rich soils in temperate forests, preferring dappled light and moisture-retentive ground.
native_region: Europe, western Asia, and North Africa
energetic_quality: Cold and narcotic with a seductive, darkly luminous quality that draws consciousness into the twilight realm between waking and dreaming.
doctrine_of_signatures: The lustrous black berries resemble dilated pupils—and indeed, the plant dilates the pupils when applied to the eyes, hence its cosmetic use by Renaissance women (bella donna, beautiful lady). The plant named for death wears the mask of beauty, teaching that poison and medicine share one face.
safety_notes: EXTREMELY TOXIC. All parts of the plant are poisonous, especially the attractive berries which have caused many accidental poisonings in children. Causes delirium, tachycardia, hyperthermia, seizures, respiratory failure, and death. Atropine is the primary toxin. No safe dose exists for self-administration.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Anticholinergic, antispasmodic, bronchodilator, mydriatic, analgesic
parts.notes: Leaves are the most commonly used part in pharmaceutical preparations. Atropine extracted from belladonna remains an essential medicine in emergency and surgical settings.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Narcotic, analgesic, antispasmodic, deliriant
parts.notes: The root was the primary ingredient in the legendary flying ointments of medieval European witchcraft, applied transdermally to mucous membranes.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Deliriant, toxic, anticholinergic
parts.notes: The attractive black berries are the most common source of accidental poisoning. Sweet-tasting, which increases danger to children.

planet_associations.notes: Saturn governs belladonna's lethal nature, its association with Atropos and the cutting of life's thread, and its role in the darkest rites of European folk magic.
Pluto reflects belladonna's underworld associations, its transformative power through encounter with death, and its role in witchcraft traditions of spirit flight.

zodiac_associations.notes: Scorpio rules the death-rebirth mysteries, seduction, and the hidden power that belladonna embodies in both its beauty and its lethality.
Capricorn mirrors belladonna's Saturnian authority, the gravity of its consequences, and the severe initiatory lessons it teaches about the boundaries between medicine and poison.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Belladonna plasters and ointments were standard analgesic preparations in European medicine for centuries, applied topically for muscular and neuralgic pain.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: External topical use only in historical context; pharmaceutical atropine is now used in clinical settings instead.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Atropine's bronchodilator action was historically used for asthma and whooping cough; it remains relevant in modern emergency medicine.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Pharmaceutical atropine preparations only; self-dosing with plant material is never safe.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: The antispasmodic action of atropine relieves intestinal cramping and colic; this remains a recognized pharmaceutical application.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Pharmaceutical preparations only; belladonna tincture was historically dosed in drops but has been replaced by standardized atropine.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Central ingredient in the flying ointments of medieval European witchcraft, applied transdermally to induce out-of-body experiences and spirit flight.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Historical use only in traditional witchcraft context; no modern application recommended due to extreme toxicity.

contraindication_associations.reason: Atropine and scopolamine readily cross the placenta causing fetal tachycardia and anticholinergic effects; associated with neonatal meconium ileus
contraindication_associations.notes: All tropane alkaloids are contraindicated in pregnancy; fetal anticholinergic syndrome is well-documented

contraindication_associations.reason: Atropine is a competitive muscarinic antagonist that eliminates vagal tone, producing uncontrolled tachycardia and increasing risk of ventricular fibrillation
contraindication_associations.notes: Even therapeutic atropine doses require cardiac monitoring; crude plant preparations have unpredictable alkaloid content

contraindication_associations.reason: Central anticholinergic syndrome produces florid delirium with formed hallucinations, severe agitation, and complete dissociation from reality for 24-72 hours
contraindication_associations.notes: Belladonna poisoning is clinically indistinguishable from acute psychosis; may cause lasting psychological trauma

contraindication_associations.reason: High-dose tropane alkaloid exposure causes CNS excitation progressing to convulsions as part of the anticholinergic toxidrome
contraindication_associations.notes: Seizures are a medical emergency in anticholinergic poisoning; may be resistant to standard anticonvulsants

contraindication_associations.reason: Strong anticholinergic effects impair GI motility and reduce protective mucus secretion while altering gastric pH regulation
contraindication_associations.notes: May worsen gastric ulceration despite symptom-masking reduction in acid secretion

contraindication_associations.reason: Atropine-induced tachycardia increases cardiac output and systolic blood pressure; loss of parasympathetic regulation destabilizes blood pressure control
contraindication_associations.notes: Blood pressure dysregulation is unpredictable with crude plant preparations; avoid in hypertensive patients

description: The silver-grey herb of Artemis, goddess of the wild and the moon, wormwood is the bitter muse of poets and visionaries, the green fairy of absinthe, and an ancient vermifuge of exceptional potency. Its intensely bitter taste is the signature of a plant that purifies by confrontation.
habitat: Dry, rocky, and disturbed ground across temperate regions, thriving in poor soils, roadsides, and waste places in full sun.
native_region: Europe, temperate Asia, and North Africa
energetic_quality: Cooling and drying, with an intensely bitter, clarifying quality that cuts through stagnation and fog with lunar precision.
doctrine_of_signatures: The silvery, moonlit foliage connects wormwood to Artemis and lunar consciousness. Its extreme bitterness is the taste of truth that purifies by making the unpalatable unavoidable—bitter medicine for bitter conditions.
safety_notes: Thujone is neurotoxic in large doses, causing seizures and organ damage. Essential oil should never be ingested. Contraindicated in pregnancy, seizure disorders, and kidney disease. Modern absinthe contains regulated thujone levels. Short-term use only for medicinal purposes.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Vermifuge, bitter digestive tonic, cholagogue, antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory
parts.notes: The silvery leaves and flowering tops are the primary medicinal material, used as a bitter digestive, antiparasitic, and the base of absinthe.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Aromatic bitter, carminative, antimicrobial, mild nervine
parts.notes: The small yellow flower heads concentrate the essential oils and are preferred for tea and tincture preparations.

planet_associations.notes: The Moon governs wormwood through its connection to Artemis, its silver foliage, its influence on dreams and intuition, and its traditional use in lunar ritual.
Mars reflects wormwood's fierce bitterness, its aggressive antiparasitic action, and its capacity to cut through stagnation with surgical precision.

zodiac_associations.notes: Cancer resonates with wormwood's lunar rulership, its action on the digestive system (Cancer's domain), and its connection to the protective, purifying aspects of the Moon.
Scorpio mirrors wormwood's association with transformation through purification, its antiparasitic action, and its role in confronting what festers in darkness.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: One of the oldest and most reliable vermifuges in the European and Middle Eastern herbal pharmacopoeia, effective against intestinal worms and other parasites.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2g dried herb as tea, taken for 7-10 days maximum; not for prolonged use due to thujone content.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: The extreme bitterness stimulates gastric secretions, bile flow, and peristalsis, making it one of the most powerful digestive bitters known.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 10-30 drops of tincture before meals; the bitter taste on the tongue is essential to the mechanism of action.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Thujone's mild psychoactive effects have long been associated with enhanced dream vividness and prophetic dreaming in European folk tradition.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Mild infusion of dried herb before sleep; mugwort (A. vulgaris) is preferred for dream work as it contains less thujone.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: The clarifying, cutting quality of wormwood's bitterness has been used to sharpen mental focus and dispel confusion in traditional European herbalism.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Small doses of tincture (5-15 drops); the sensory impact of the bitterness is considered part of the therapeutic action.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Clinical studies show Artemisia absinthium can induce and maintain remission in Crohn's disease and reduce need for steroids.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 500 mg dried herb 3 times daily, or 2-3 ml tincture. Use under practitioner guidance.

contraindication_associations.reason: Thujone is a GABA-A receptor antagonist with abortifacient properties; wormwood has been historically used as an emmenagogue and abortifacient
contraindication_associations.notes: Traditional abortifacient use is well-documented; thujone stimulates uterine contractions

contraindication_associations.reason: Thujone blocks GABA-A receptors reducing inhibitory neurotransmission and directly lowers seizure threshold; historically associated with absinthism convulsions
contraindication_associations.notes: GABA-A antagonism makes thujone a proconvulsant; absolutely avoid with epilepsy or seizure history

contraindication_associations.reason: Thujone and other terpenoids in wormwood are hepatotoxic; chronic use causes liver damage through oxidative stress and CYP450 interaction
contraindication_associations.notes: Monitor liver function with any use; hepatotoxicity documented in absinthe-related liver damage

contraindication_associations.reason: Wormwood contains nephrotoxic terpenoids; thujone metabolites are renally excreted and may accumulate with renal impairment
contraindication_associations.notes: Renal clearance of thujone metabolites is important for preventing accumulation

contraindication_associations.reason: Wormwood contains bitter sesquiterpene lactones that stimulate gastric acid secretion, potentially aggravating existing ulceration
contraindication_associations.notes: Bitter principles increase gastric acid output; may worsen peptic and duodenal ulcers

description: The opium of the poor, wild lettuce exudes a bitter milky latex that has served as a gentle sedative and pain reliever for those without access to stronger medicines since ancient Egypt. It is the humble garden plant's wilder, more potent cousin, offering quiet relief without the chains of addiction.
habitat: Roadsides, waste ground, field margins, and rocky hillsides across temperate regions, preferring dry, well-drained soils in full sun to partial shade.
energetic_quality: Cooling and moistening, with a gentle, milky, sedating quality that soothes the nervous system like a whispered lullaby.
doctrine_of_signatures: The milky white latex that flows when the stem is cut visually resembles opium poppy sap, and indeed serves a parallel though gentler analgesic function. The milk also suggests the nurturing, sleep-inducing quality of mother's milk.
safety_notes: Generally well-tolerated at traditional doses. Large doses of the latex can cause nausea, dizziness, and anxiety. May cause allergic reactions in those sensitive to Asteraceae. The raw latex can cause skin irritation. Not a substitute for medical pain management in serious conditions.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Sedative, analgesic, anxiolytic, antitussive, hypnotic
parts.notes: The dried milky latex (lactucarium) is the primary medicine, historically collected by scoring the flowering stalks. It was listed in pharmacopoeias as a mild opium substitute.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Mild sedative, nutritive, anti-inflammatory, diuretic
parts.notes: Leaves can be used as tea or tincture for milder sedative effects. The lower leaves of flowering plants contain more active compounds.

planet_associations.notes: The Moon governs wild lettuce's milky, sedative, sleep-inducing qualities, its connection to dreams, and its gentle, nurturing mode of healing.
Saturn reflects wild lettuce's analgesic properties, its bitter latex, and its historical role as the poor person's pain medicine—Saturn's domain of limitation and endurance.

zodiac_associations.notes: Cancer embodies wild lettuce's lunar, milky, nurturing quality, its connection to sleep and dreams, and its gentle, protective mode of soothing pain and anxiety.
Pisces resonates with wild lettuce's dreamy sedation, its capacity to soften the harsh edges of reality, and its gentle dissolution of pain and tension.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Lactucarium has been used since antiquity as a gentle sleep aid, offering sedation without the dependency risks of opiate-based remedies.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Tincture of lactucarium, 1-2ml before bed; or dried latex dissolved in warm water.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: The mild anxiolytic action provides gentle calming without significant sedation at moderate doses, suitable for daytime nervousness.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2g dried leaf as tea, 2-3 times daily; may be combined with other calming herbs.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Known historically as lettuce opium for its analgesic properties; provides mild pain relief suitable for minor to moderate discomfort.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Tincture of lactucarium, 1-3ml as needed; not a substitute for medical pain management in severe conditions.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: The antitussive properties of lactucin make wild lettuce useful for dry, irritable coughs, similar to codeine's cough-suppressant action but much milder.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Small doses of tincture (0.5-1ml) as needed for cough; may be combined with other respiratory herbs.

contraindication_associations.reason: Lactucarium contains sesquiterpene lactones with mild sedative and analgesic properties; safety in pregnancy has not been established
contraindication_associations.notes: Insufficient safety data; avoid as a precaution due to uterine stimulant potential of sesquiterpene lactones

contraindication_associations.reason: Member of the Asteraceae family; contains sesquiterpene lactones that are potent contact sensitizers and can trigger allergic reactions in susceptible individuals
contraindication_associations.notes: Cross-reactivity with ragweed, chamomile, and other Asteraceae plants; may cause contact dermatitis or systemic allergic response

contraindication_associations.reason: Lactucarium has mild sedative and psychoactive effects; may potentiate CNS depressants and interfere with psychiatric medication efficacy
contraindication_associations.notes: Mild CNS effects but caution advised with concurrent use of sedatives, anxiolytics, or antidepressants

contraindication_associations.reason: Bioactive sesquiterpene lactones may be excreted in breast milk; safety for nursing infants has not been evaluated
contraindication_associations.notes: Insufficient safety data for lactation; avoid as a precaution

description: The great grandfather plant of the Americas, Tobacco has served as the supreme offering and prayer carrier in Indigenous ceremony for millennia. Its sharp, clarifying smoke cuts through energetic fog and establishes direct communication between the human and spirit worlds.
habitat: Cultivated in warm, well-drained soils with full sun exposure. Thrives in subtropical and warm temperate climates with rich, loamy earth.
energetic_quality: Warming, drying, intensely clarifying. Sharpens focus and drives energy upward and outward with commanding force.
doctrine_of_signatures: The large, broad leaves that reach upward like open hands suggest the plant's role as an offering to the sky and spirits above. The sticky resin coating the leaves reflects its ability to bind and hold intention.
safety_notes: Highly addictive when used recreationally. Nicotine is a potent neurotoxin in large doses. Ceremonial use involves vastly different intention and dosage than habitual smoking. Contraindicated in pregnancy, cardiovascular disease, and hypertension. Green tobacco sickness can occur from skin absorption during harvest.

parts.typical_compounds: Nicotine, nornicotine, anabasine, beta-caryophyllene, rutin, chlorogenic acid
parts.therapeutic_properties: Stimulant, nootropic, emetic (in high doses), parasiticide, ceremonial offering, prayer carrier
parts.notes: The primary ceremonial and medicinal part. Smoked, chewed, snuffed, or used as offerings in Indigenous traditions. Small ceremonial doses differ profoundly from habitual recreational use.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Subtle nervine, vibrational medicine, gentle opening
parts.notes: The trumpet-shaped flowers are used in flower essences for addiction recovery and reconnecting with the sacred dimension of tobacco. Far gentler than the leaf.

planet_associations.notes: Tobacco's commanding, fiery nature and its capacity to sharpen the will and cut through confusion align with Martian energy. Its use as a warrior plant and protector in Indigenous traditions reinforces this association.
The plant's dual nature as both healer and destroyer, and its deep association with death, transformation, and ancestral communication connect it to Plutonian themes of power and shadow.

zodiac_associations.notes: Scorpio governs transformation, death, and the unseen realms. Tobacco's role as a bridge between worlds and its potent dual nature as medicine and poison embody Scorpio's intensity.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Ceremonial tobacco smoke carries prayers and opens dialogue with the spirit world. Used across the Americas as the primary vehicle for sacred communication and offering.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Small pinch smoked in a sacred pipe with clear intention. Ceremonial use only; not for habitual consumption.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Nicotine's acute effects on acetylcholine receptors sharpen focus, enhance mental clarity, and cut through confusion or indecision.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Single ceremonial smoke or small quid held in the lip. Intention and moderation are essential.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Nicotine is a potent vermifuge. Traditional use as an antiparasitic in both human and veterinary folk medicine across the Americas and Asia.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Weak decoction used externally or in very small internal doses under experienced guidance only. Toxicity risk is significant.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: In high ceremonial doses, tobacco induces altered states, visions, and communion with plant spirits. Central to Amazonian curanderismo and North American pipe ceremony.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Large doses used only under guidance of experienced ceremonial leaders. Purging (vomiting) is common and considered part of the cleansing process.

contraindication_associations.reason: Nicotine is a potent vasoconstrictor that reduces placental blood flow causing fetal growth restriction, preterm birth, and increased risk of stillbirth
contraindication_associations.notes: Nicotine exposure causes measurable fetal harm including low birth weight and sudden infant death syndrome risk

contraindication_associations.reason: Nicotine activates the sympathetic nervous system causing tachycardia, vasoconstriction, increased myocardial oxygen demand, and accelerated atherosclerosis
contraindication_associations.notes: Tobacco use is a primary modifiable risk factor for cardiovascular disease and myocardial infarction

contraindication_associations.reason: Nicotine causes acute increases in blood pressure through catecholamine release and sympathetic activation; chronic use promotes arterial stiffness
contraindication_associations.notes: Each cigarette produces acute blood pressure elevation; chronic use contributes to sustained hypertension

contraindication_associations.reason: Nicotine increases gastric acid secretion, reduces bicarbonate production, and impairs mucosal blood flow, all of which delay ulcer healing
contraindication_associations.notes: Tobacco use significantly delays peptic ulcer healing and increases recurrence rates

contraindication_associations.reason: Nicotine is readily excreted in breast milk at concentrations proportional to maternal plasma; reduces milk production via dopaminergic suppression of prolactin
contraindication_associations.notes: Infant exposure through breast milk causes irritability, poor feeding, and sleep disturbance; reduces milk supply

description: Named 'Food of the Gods' by Linnaeus himself, Cacao is the ancient heart-opening sacrament of Mesoamerican civilization. Its rich, bitter medicine floods the body with bliss chemistry while dissolving the armoring around the emotional heart.
habitat: Tropical rainforest understory, requiring shade, warmth, humidity, and rich volcanic soils. Grows within 20 degrees of the equator.
energetic_quality: Warming, moistening, heart-opening. Moves blood and emotion with gentle but persistent force, softening rigidity in body and psyche.
doctrine_of_signatures: The cacao pod grows directly from the trunk, close to the tree's heartwood, reflecting its affinity for the human heart. The seeds are nestled in sweet mucilage like blood cells within the chambers of the heart.
safety_notes: Generally safe in food and ceremonial doses. High doses may trigger migraines in sensitive individuals. Theobromine is a cardiac stimulant; caution with heart conditions and certain medications. Contains tyramine and phenylethylamine; caution with MAOIs. May affect calcium absorption with excessive use.

parts.typical_compounds: Theobromine, anandamide, phenylethylamine, catechins, procyanidins, magnesium, tryptophan
parts.therapeutic_properties: Cardiac tonic, vasodilator, mood elevator, antioxidant, heart opener, gentle stimulant, empathenogen
parts.notes: The fermented and roasted seed (cacao bean) is the primary medicine. Ceremonial-grade cacao is minimally processed to preserve the full spectrum of psychoactive and nutritive compounds.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Emollient, skin nourishing, carrier for other medicines
parts.notes: Cacao butter extracted from the seed serves as a carrier for topical preparations and suppositories. Its melting point near body temperature makes it an ideal delivery vehicle.

planet_associations.notes: Venus governs love, beauty, pleasure, and the heart. Cacao is the supreme heart-opening plant, flooding the body with the chemistry of love and dissolving emotional barriers.
The warming, vitalizing, and golden qualities of cacao connect it to solar energy. It was considered a solar sacrament by the Aztecs and Mayans.

zodiac_associations.notes: Taurus, ruled by Venus, governs sensual pleasure, embodiment, and the earthly delights. Cacao engages all the senses and anchors spiritual experience in the body.
Leo rules the physical heart. Cacao's direct action on cardiac function, blood flow, and emotional heart-opening resonate with Leo's generous, warm-hearted nature.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Anandamide, phenylethylamine, and theobromine create a natural mood elevation. Magnesium content supports neurotransmitter production and nervous system function.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Ceremonial dose: 28-42g raw cacao paste dissolved in hot water. Daily supportive dose: 15-20g. Use ceremonial-grade, minimally processed cacao.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Theobromine is a mild, sustained cardiac tonic and vasodilator. High magnesium and flavanol content support healthy heart rhythm and vascular tone.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Moderate daily doses of 15-20g raw cacao. Avoid excessive doses in those with arrhythmias; theobromine is stimulating in high amounts.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Heart-opening ceremony with cacao facilitates the release of stored grief and emotional armoring. The combination of empathogenic compounds softens the protective walls around the heart.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Full ceremonial dose of 42g raw cacao paste in warm water, taken with intention in a supported ceremonial setting.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Theobromine and procyanidins promote vasodilation and improve peripheral blood flow. Clinical evidence supports cacao flavanols for endothelial function and blood pressure.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 20-30g ceremonial-grade cacao daily, or standardized flavanol supplement as directed.

contraindication_associations.reason: Contains caffeine and theobromine which cross the placenta; high intake associated with increased miscarriage risk and fetal growth restriction
contraindication_associations.notes: Moderate consumption is generally considered safe; high-dose ceremonial cacao preparations should be avoided

contraindication_associations.reason: Theobromine and caffeine are methylxanthine stimulants that increase heart rate and cardiac output; high ceremonial doses may provoke arrhythmias
contraindication_associations.notes: Normal dietary amounts are generally safe; concentrated ceremonial cacao contains high methylxanthine doses that stress the cardiovascular system

contraindication_associations.reason: Theobromine and caffeine stimulate gastric acid secretion; cacao also relaxes the lower esophageal sphincter promoting acid reflux
contraindication_associations.notes: May aggravate GERD and peptic ulcers; avoid concentrated cacao preparations with active ulceration

contraindication_associations.reason: Contains phenylethylamine, anandamide, and caffeine which modulate monoamine neurotransmission; high-dose ceremonial preparations may trigger anxiety and agitation
contraindication_associations.notes: Relevant primarily for ceremonial doses; may interact with MAO inhibitors due to tyramine and phenylethylamine content

description: A golden-flowered shrub of the Mexican highlands, Damiana has been prized for centuries as an aphrodisiac, nervine, and subtle euphoric that kindles the fire of desire and creative expression. Its warm, resinous aroma carries the energy of solar passion tempered by gentle relaxation.
habitat: Dry, rocky hillsides and open scrublands of subtropical and tropical regions. Prefers well-drained, sandy soils with full sun.
native_region: Mexico, Central America, Caribbean, Southern Texas
energetic_quality: Warming, drying, stimulating yet relaxing. Kindles the fire of the lower dan tian while simultaneously easing nervous tension.
doctrine_of_signatures: The small golden flowers radiating warmth and sweet fragrance speak to Damiana's affinity for the sacral and solar plexus centers. The aromatic, resinous leaves suggest its warming, stimulating quality.
safety_notes: Generally well-tolerated. May interact with diabetic medications due to hypoglycemic effects. Avoid excessive doses during pregnancy. High doses may cause mild laxative effects. Long-term excessive use may affect iron absorption.

parts.typical_compounds: Apigenin, arbutin, damianin, beta-caryophyllene, thymol, tannins, volatile oils
parts.therapeutic_properties: Aphrodisiac, nervine, mild euphoric, antidepressant, urinary antiseptic, digestive tonic
parts.notes: The primary medicinal part. Traditionally smoked for mild euphoria and relaxation, or taken as tea and tincture for aphrodisiac and nervine effects. The dried leaf has a distinctive sweet, fig-like aroma.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Mild astringent, supportive tonic
parts.notes: The stems are included in traditional preparations but contain lower concentrations of active compounds than the leaves. Often used in combination with the leaf in whole-plant preparations.

planet_associations.notes: Venus governs love, desire, pleasure, and the reproductive organs. Damiana's renowned aphrodisiac properties and its ability to kindle warmth in the pelvis place it firmly under Venusian rulership.
The stimulating, warming, and activating qualities of Damiana carry Martian energy, particularly in its capacity to kindle drive and assertive desire.

zodiac_associations.notes: Scorpio rules the reproductive organs and sexual energy. Damiana's deep action on libido, pelvic blood flow, and sexual vitality resonate with Scorpio's domain.
Libra, also ruled by Venus, governs partnership and relational harmony. Damiana supports connection, sensuality, and the relaxation of social and romantic inhibition.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Long-standing traditional reputation as a sexual tonic for both men and women. May improve blood flow to the pelvic region and modulate hormonal balance.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 30-60 drops of tincture (1:5 ratio), 2-3 times daily. Effects are cumulative over 2-4 weeks. Often combined with other aphrodisiac herbs.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Apigenin and other flavonoids provide gentle anxiolytic effects. Damiana calms the nervous system without sedation, promoting a state of relaxed alertness.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2 teaspoons dried leaf per cup, steeped 10-15 minutes. Drink 2-3 times daily.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Mild euphoric effects when smoked provide temporary mood elevation. The combination of nervine and stimulant properties helps lift stagnant emotional states.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Small pinch smoked alone or blended with other herbs. Use mindfully and occasionally; not a substitute for comprehensive mental health care.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Bitter and aromatic principles stimulate digestive secretions and relieve sluggishness. Warming action helps move stagnant food and energy through the gut.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1 teaspoon dried leaf per cup, steeped 10 minutes. Drink before meals.

contraindication_associations.reason: Damiana has traditional use as a uterine stimulant and emmenagogue; flavonoids and terpenoids may promote uterine contractions
contraindication_associations.notes: Traditional use as an emmenagogue suggests uterotonic activity; avoid during pregnancy

contraindication_associations.reason: Damiana contains compounds that may lower blood glucose levels, potentially causing hypoglycemia when combined with antidiabetic medications
contraindication_associations.notes: Monitor blood sugar closely if using with diabetes medications; may require dose adjustment

contraindication_associations.reason: Bioactive terpenoids and flavonoids may be excreted in breast milk; no safety data available for nursing infants
contraindication_associations.notes: Insufficient safety data; avoid during breastfeeding as a precaution

contraindication_associations.reason: Damiana contains arbutin and other compounds metabolized hepatically; potential for hepatotoxicity with impaired liver function
contraindication_associations.notes: Limited hepatotoxicity data but caution warranted with compromised liver function

description: One of the oldest medicinal plants in continuous use, Ma Huang has anchored Chinese herbal medicine for over five thousand years as the supreme opener of the lungs and sinuses. Its stark, leafless stems rising from desert sands embody the raw, unadorned power of the breath itself.
habitat: Arid and semi-arid desert regions, rocky slopes, and sandy plains. Thrives in harsh, dry conditions with extreme temperature variation.
energetic_quality: Warming, drying, strongly dispersing. Opens the surface and drives energy powerfully upward and outward, releasing exterior conditions.
doctrine_of_signatures: The hollow, jointed stems resemble bronchial tubes and airways, pointing directly to Ephedra's primary action on the respiratory system. The plant's ability to thrive in harsh, arid environments reflects its drying, clearing nature.
safety_notes: Potent sympathomimetic; serious cardiovascular risks including hypertension, arrhythmia, stroke, and heart attack. Banned in many countries as a dietary supplement. Contraindicated with MAOIs, stimulants, cardiovascular medications. Not for use in hypertension, heart disease, anxiety, hyperthyroidism, or pregnancy. Traditional Chinese medicine uses carefully controlled doses in formula context only.

parts.typical_compounds: Ephedrine, pseudoephedrine, norephedrine, tannins, saponins, volatile oils
parts.therapeutic_properties: Bronchodilator, decongestant, sympathomimetic, diaphoretic, thermogenic, anti-asthmatic
parts.notes: The aerial stems (Ma Huang) are the primary medicinal part. Always used in formula context in traditional Chinese medicine, never as a single herb. The root (Ma Huang Gen) has opposite action and stops sweating.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Astringent, antiperspirant, stabilizing
parts.notes: Interestingly, the root has the opposite action of the stem: it stops sweating rather than promoting it. Used in Chinese medicine for spontaneous sweating and night sweats.

planet_associations.notes: The intensely stimulating, heating, and driving nature of Ephedra is quintessentially Martian. It mobilizes the body's defenses with aggressive force, opening blockages and dispersing stagnation.

zodiac_associations.notes: Aries rules the head and sinuses, Ephedra's primary target. The plant's forceful, initiating energy and its capacity to clear blockages resonate with Aries' pioneering nature.
Sagittarius governs expansion and the lungs in traditional astrology. Ephedra's powerful bronchodilating action and its capacity to open respiratory passages align with Sagittarian expansiveness.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Ephedrine relaxes bronchial smooth muscle and opens airways. One of the most effective botanical bronchodilators known, with a five-thousand-year track record in Chinese medicine.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 2-6g dried stem decocted in formula (never alone) for 10-15 minutes. Always used under qualified TCM practitioner guidance.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Releases the exterior and promotes sweating to resolve the initial stage of wind-cold invasion. The classic Ma Huang Tang formula has been used for thousands of years for this purpose.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Part of classical formula Ma Huang Tang: 6g Ma Huang, 4g Gui Zhi, 9g Xing Ren, 3g Zhi Gan Cao. Decoct and take in divided doses.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Sympathomimetic action provides powerful stimulation of the central nervous system and adrenals. Historically used to combat exhaustion in traditional and folk medicine contexts.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Low-dose formulas only under practitioner supervision. Not appropriate for long-term use due to adrenal depletion risk.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Pseudoephedrine reduces nasal congestion and sinus pressure. Ephedrine has antihistamine-like effects that reduce allergic rhinitis symptoms.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Used in formula context at 3-6g dried stem. Short-term use only. Requires practitioner guidance.

contraindication_associations.reason: Ephedrine and pseudoephedrine are sympathomimetic amines that cross the placenta, causing fetal tachycardia and reduced uterine blood flow
contraindication_associations.notes: Vasoconstriction reduces placental perfusion; associated with fetal distress and low birth weight

contraindication_associations.reason: Ephedrine directly stimulates beta-1 adrenergic receptors increasing heart rate, contractility, and cardiac output; causes coronary vasospasm and arrhythmias
contraindication_associations.notes: Multiple fatalities from ephedra-related cardiac events documented; FDA banned ephedra-containing supplements in 2004 due to cardiovascular deaths

contraindication_associations.reason: Ephedrine and pseudoephedrine cause potent alpha-1 adrenergic vasoconstriction and norepinephrine release, producing acute and sustained blood pressure elevation
contraindication_associations.notes: Hypertensive emergencies documented; absolutely contraindicated with existing hypertension or concurrent MAO inhibitor use

contraindication_associations.reason: Sympathomimetic overstimulation of the CNS can lower seizure threshold; seizures reported in ephedra toxicity cases
contraindication_associations.notes: CNS stimulation from ephedrine can provoke seizures; documented in adverse event reports

contraindication_associations.reason: Ephedrine is a CNS stimulant that can trigger anxiety, agitation, psychosis, and mania through catecholamine excess
contraindication_associations.notes: Stimulant psychosis reported with high doses; avoid with anxiety disorders, bipolar disorder, and psychotic conditions

contraindication_associations.reason: Ephedrine stimulates hepatic glycogenolysis and reduces insulin sensitivity through beta-adrenergic activation, disrupting glucose homeostasis
contraindication_associations.notes: May cause hyperglycemia and interfere with diabetic medication management; monitor blood sugar carefully

description: The warm, intoxicating seed that once commanded the price of gold, Nutmeg conceals within its aromatic flesh a potent psychoactive compound capable of inducing prolonged visionary states. Its dual nature as common kitchen spice and powerful deliriant reflects the hidden depths that ordinary things may contain.
habitat: Tropical rainforest environments with rich volcanic soils, high humidity, and consistent warmth. Thrives in maritime tropical climates near sea level.
energetic_quality: Warming, drying, stimulating in small doses, intoxicating in large doses. Carries a deceptive gentleness that conceals deep psychoactive power.
doctrine_of_signatures: The brain-like convolutions on the seed surface point to Nutmeg's action on consciousness and the mind. The mace surrounding the seed like a red web suggests its capacity to entangle the senses.
safety_notes: Psychoactive doses (5g+) can cause severe toxicity: tachycardia, nausea, hallucinations, delirium, organ damage, and potentially death. Effects are delayed 4-8 hours and last 24-72 hours. Never use recreationally. Culinary doses (pinch to 1g) are safe. Contraindicated in pregnancy, liver disease, and psychiatric conditions. Dangerous interactions with MAOIs.

parts.typical_compounds: Myristicin, elemicin, safrole, eugenol, alpha-pinene, limonene, linalool
parts.therapeutic_properties: Carminative, digestive stimulant, anti-inflammatory, oneirogen, deliriant (high dose), analgesic
parts.notes: The dried seed kernel is the primary spice and medicine. Myristicin is converted to MMDA (a psychoactive amphetamine analog) in the body. Culinary doses are therapeutic; psychoactive doses are dangerous.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Topical analgesic, anti-inflammatory, emollient
parts.notes: Nutmeg butter (trimyristin) is extracted from the seed and used topically for joint pain and inflammation. Also serves as a carrier in traditional balms and ointments.

planet_associations.notes: Neptune governs altered states, dreams, illusion, and the dissolution of ordinary consciousness. Nutmeg's capacity to induce prolonged, dreamlike intoxication and its deceptive nature align with Neptunian energy.
Jupiter governs abundance and the liver. Nutmeg's historical role as a precious spice of commerce and its warming, expansive digestive properties carry Jupiterian overtones.

zodiac_associations.notes: Pisces governs dreams, altered states, and the dissolution of boundaries between self and cosmos. Nutmeg's oneirogenic and psychoactive properties resonate with Piscean dissolution.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Warming carminative action stimulates digestive fire and relieves gas, bloating, and nausea. A staple digestive spice in Ayurvedic and Unani medicine.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Pinch to 1/4 teaspoon freshly grated nutmeg in warm milk or food. Culinary doses only.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Small doses promote relaxation and sleep onset. Traditional Ayurvedic remedy of nutmeg in warm milk before bed has gentle sedative action through myristicin's subtle effect on serotonin pathways.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Pinch of freshly grated nutmeg in warm milk, 30 minutes before bed. Never exceed 1/4 teaspoon.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Myristicin and elemicin have oneirogenic activity, promoting vivid and memorable dreams. Low doses can enhance dream vividness without the dangers of psychoactive doses.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Pinch of freshly grated nutmeg in warm milk before sleep. Stay well within culinary dose range.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Topical application of nutmeg oil or butter reduces inflammation and pain in joints and muscles. Eugenol and other volatile oils provide analgesic action.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Apply nutmeg butter or diluted essential oil topically to affected joints 2-3 times daily. For external use only.

contraindication_associations.reason: Myristicin is a monoamine oxidase inhibitor and psychoactive compound that crosses the placenta; high doses have abortifacient properties through uterine stimulation
contraindication_associations.notes: Culinary amounts are safe; psychoactive doses (5+ grams) are contraindicated due to abortifacient and toxic effects

contraindication_associations.reason: Myristicin and elemicin are metabolized into amphetamine-like compounds (MMDA analogs) that produce hallucinations, delirium, and psychosis at high doses
contraindication_associations.notes: Nutmeg intoxication produces anticholinergic-like delirium lasting 24-72 hours; may trigger lasting psychological disturbance

contraindication_associations.reason: Myristicin undergoes extensive hepatic metabolism; hepatotoxicity documented at psychoactive doses with elevated liver enzymes and hepatocellular damage
contraindication_associations.notes: Toxic doses place significant metabolic burden on the liver; avoid high-dose use with hepatic impairment

contraindication_associations.reason: Myristicin and related compounds produce sympathomimetic effects including tachycardia, palpitations, and blood pressure fluctuations at psychoactive doses
contraindication_associations.notes: Cardiovascular symptoms are common in nutmeg intoxication; avoid high-dose use with cardiac conditions

contraindication_associations.reason: Nutmeg intoxication can cause convulsions through a combination of serotonergic, anticholinergic, and sympathomimetic mechanisms
contraindication_associations.notes: Seizures documented in nutmeg poisoning case reports; risk is dose-dependent

description: A towering leguminous tree of the South American savannas, Yopo yields seeds that have been ritually snuffed for over four thousand years to induce rapid, intense visionary communion with the spirit world. The seeds, roasted and ground into a fine powder, deliver one of the most powerful and ancient entheogenic experiences known to humanity.
habitat: Tropical savannas, grasslands, and open forests of South America. Prefers well-drained soils in warm, seasonally dry climates.
energetic_quality: Intensely heating, explosive, visionary. Shatters ordinary perception with rapid force and catapults awareness into other dimensions of being.
doctrine_of_signatures: The flat, dark seeds within their curving pods resemble eyes peering from within, suggesting the inner vision this plant awakens. The tree's immense height points to the elevated states of consciousness it facilitates.
safety_notes: Extremely powerful psychoactive; produces intense physical effects including severe nasal burning, purging, and transient cardiovascular stress. Never use alone or without experienced ceremonial guidance. Contraindicated with MAOIs (though traditionally combined with them), cardiovascular conditions, psychiatric conditions, and pregnancy. Legal status varies by jurisdiction.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Entheogenic, visionary, purgative, consciousness-expanding
parts.notes: Seeds are toasted, dehusked, and ground into a fine snuff powder, often mixed with calcium hydroxide (lime) or snail shell ash to activate the alkaloids. Administered nasally via a ceremonial blowpipe.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Astringent, antimicrobial, secondary entheogenic source
parts.notes: The bark contains some of the same tryptamines as the seeds but in lower concentrations. Historically used in some preparations but less common than the seed snuff.

planet_associations.notes: Uranus governs sudden breakthrough, lightning-flash insight, and the shattering of ordinary reality. Yopo's rapid, explosive onset and its capacity to catapult consciousness into radically altered states embody Uranian energy.

zodiac_associations.notes: Aquarius, ruled by Uranus, governs visionary states and radical shifts in perception. Yopo's capacity to deliver instantaneous visionary experiences resonates with Aquarian breakthrough consciousness.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Among the most powerful known entheogens, Yopo snuff produces rapid onset visionary states lasting 15-30 minutes. Used for millennia in South American shamanic traditions for spirit communication and healing.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Dosage varies by preparation and individual tolerance. Always administered by experienced ceremonial leader. Start with very small amounts.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Ceremonial use facilitates direct encounter with the sacred and reconnection to spiritual dimensions of existence. The intense purging is considered an essential spiritual cleansing.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Ceremonial context only. Preparation and dosage determined by experienced practitioner.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: The tryptamine compounds may facilitate processing of traumatic memories through altered state therapy, similar to emerging research on related psychedelics.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Only in supported ceremonial settings with experienced guides. Integration support essential.

contraindication_associations.reason: Contains bufotenin and DMT, potent 5-HT2A serotonin receptor agonists that can trigger psychotic episodes, severe anxiety, and exacerbate schizophrenia or bipolar disorder
contraindication_associations.notes: Absolutely contraindicated in individuals with psychotic disorders, schizophrenia, or bipolar disorder. Risk of prolonged dissociative states and psychological decompensation.

contraindication_associations.reason: Bufotenin has strong vasoconstrictive and hypertensive effects mediated through serotonin and adrenergic receptor activation, increasing cardiac workload and risk of arrhythmia
contraindication_associations.notes: Can cause dangerous spikes in blood pressure and heart rate. Avoid entirely with any cardiovascular condition including arrhythmias and coronary artery disease.

contraindication_associations.reason: Bufotenin and 5-MeO-DMT induce acute hypertension through peripheral vasoconstriction and sympathomimetic activity
