ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 300 mg standardized extract twice daily

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Anti-inflammatory action of escin reduces swelling and tissue damage in venous disease
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 300 mg standardized extract twice daily

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Aescin strengthens blood vessel walls and improves venous return, supporting overall peripheral vascular health.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 300 mg extract standardized to 50 mg aescin, twice daily.

contraindication_associations.reason: Horse chestnut contains aescin, a saponin compound with anti-inflammatory and venotonic properties that has insufficient safety data in pregnancy and may affect uterine blood flow
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid internal use during pregnancy; raw horse chestnut seeds contain the toxic glycoside esculin

contraindication_associations.reason: Aescin inhibits platelet aggregation and has antithrombotic activity; the coumarin constituent esculin acts as a vitamin K antagonist, compounding anticoagulant effects
contraindication_associations.notes: Contraindicated with warfarin, heparin, and other anticoagulants; discontinue at least two weeks before surgery

contraindication_associations.reason: Horse chestnut contains nephrotoxic saponins; aescin may impair glomerular filtration and exacerbate existing renal insufficiency. Raw seeds contain highly toxic esculin that can cause acute renal failure
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid in individuals with impaired kidney function; only standardized, esculin-removed preparations should be considered, and only under medical supervision

contraindication_associations.reason: Hepatic metabolism of aescin and related saponins may be impaired in liver disease, leading to toxic accumulation; esculin in unprocessed preparations is directly hepatotoxic
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid raw or unstandardized preparations; use only standardized extracts with confirmed esculin removal and monitor liver function

contraindication_associations.reason: Aescin has demonstrated hypoglycemic effects in pharmacological studies and may potentiate insulin or oral hypoglycemic agents
contraindication_associations.notes: Monitor blood sugar if using horse chestnut alongside diabetes medications

description: Cleavers is a scrambling annual herb that clings to clothing and fur with its tiny hooked hairs, found abundantly in hedgerows across Europe. It is one of the premier lymphatic herbs in Western tradition, gently stimulating lymphatic drainage and supporting the body's cleansing channels.
habitat: Hedgerows, garden edges, waste ground, and moist shaded areas throughout Europe and North America
energetic_quality: Cool and moist, gently diuretic and lymph-moving
doctrine_of_signatures: The sticky, clinging nature of the plant mirrors its affinity for the lymphatic system, where it helps move congested fluids that have become stagnant and adherent.
safety_notes: Very safe and gentle. No significant contraindications known. Best used fresh as it loses potency quickly when dried.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Lymphatic, diuretic, alterative, mild astringent
parts.notes: Fresh aerial parts yield the most potent preparations; best used as fresh juice or succus

parts.therapeutic_properties: Lymphatic, diuretic
parts.notes: The whole aerial herb including stems is used in medicine

planet_associations.notes: The Moon governs fluids, lymph, and the body's watery channels, all primary domains of cleavers' therapeutic action.

zodiac_associations.notes: Cancer's lunar rulership and association with fluids and protective barriers aligns with cleavers' gentle lymphatic and immune-supportive properties.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Gentle diuretic that promotes fluid movement through both urinary and lymphatic channels
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Fresh juice 3-5 mL three times daily, or cold infusion of fresh herb

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Lymphatic drainage supports clearance of skin conditions linked to lymphatic congestion
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 2-3 cups cold infusion of fresh herb daily

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Soothing diuretic that helps flush the urinary tract without irritation
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 3-4 cups cold infusion daily during acute episodes

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Lymphatic support enhances immune surveillance by promoting fluid circulation through lymph nodes
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 2-3 cups infusion daily as a spring tonic

contraindication_associations.reason: Cleavers (Galium aparine) has traditional use as a uterine stimulant and may promote uterine contractions
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid internal use during pregnancy, especially in concentrated tincture form

contraindication_associations.reason: Cleavers is a strong diuretic that may alter fluid and electrolyte balance, increasing the burden on compromised kidneys and potentially interacting with prescribed diuretic medications
contraindication_associations.notes: Use with caution in renal impairment; monitor potassium levels and kidney function

contraindication_associations.reason: Cleavers has mild hypoglycemic properties that may potentiate diabetic medications and alter blood glucose control
contraindication_associations.notes: Monitor blood sugar levels when introducing cleavers alongside hypoglycemic medications

description: Agrimony is a slender perennial with spikes of small yellow flowers, widely distributed across Europe and valued since antiquity as a wound herb and digestive remedy. In the Bach Flower tradition, it is the remedy for those who hide inner torment behind a cheerful facade.
habitat: Grasslands, hedgerows, field margins, and dry woodland edges across Europe and temperate Asia
energetic_quality: Warm and dry, gently astringent and tonifying
doctrine_of_signatures: The upright, orderly spike of yellow flowers suggests the plant's ability to restore order and harmony to disordered digestion and disjointed emotional states.
safety_notes: Generally very safe. Mild astringency may aggravate constipation in susceptible individuals. Avoid in cases of bile duct obstruction.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Astringent, digestive tonic, vulnerary, mild diuretic
parts.notes: Aerial parts gathered during flowering contain the highest tannin content

parts.therapeutic_properties: Nervine, emotional harmonizer, mild astringent
parts.notes: Flowers are used specifically in flower essence preparations for emotional indications

planet_associations.notes: Jupiter's beneficent, expansive energy aligns with agrimony's reputation as a healer and harmonizer of both body and spirit.

zodiac_associations.notes: Sagittarius, governed by Jupiter, corresponds to agrimony's optimistic healing energy and its liver-supportive digestive properties.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Gentle astringent tonic that improves sluggish digestion and tones the gastrointestinal lining
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2 teaspoons dried herb steeped 10 minutes, 3 cups daily

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Supports liver and gallbladder function with gentle bitter and astringent action
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2 cups infusion daily before meals

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Bach Flower Remedy for those who mask inner distress with cheerfulness, promoting honest emotional expression
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 4 drops in water four times daily

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Astringent and vulnerary properties support tissue repair when used as a wash or compress
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Strong infusion applied as a wound wash or compress

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Gentle astringent that supports healthy blood clotting and reduces bleeding tendency.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2 teaspoons dried herb per cup, steeped 10-15 minutes. Drink 2-3 cups daily.

contraindication_associations.reason: Agrimony contains tannins and has traditional use as a uterine tonic; its astringent and mild oxytocic properties warrant avoidance during pregnancy
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid internal use during pregnancy; insufficient safety data for gestational use

contraindication_associations.reason: Agrimony has complex effects on coagulation; it contains vitamin K-dependent coagulation factors but also compounds that may interfere with anticoagulant medication dosing
contraindication_associations.notes: May alter INR in patients on warfarin; monitor coagulation parameters closely

contraindication_associations.reason: Agrimony has demonstrated hypoglycemic effects in animal studies, possibly through enhancement of insulin secretion and improved peripheral glucose uptake
contraindication_associations.notes: Monitor blood sugar levels carefully when combining with diabetes medications; dose adjustment may be necessary

contraindication_associations.reason: High tannin content in agrimony may irritate damaged gastric mucosa and worsen symptoms in individuals with active peptic ulceration
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid strong decoctions in active ulcer disease; dilute tea preparations may be better tolerated

description: Wood betony is a perennial herb of European woodlands and meadows, considered one of the most important healing plants in the Anglo-Saxon and medieval European herbal traditions. It was said to cure nearly every ailment, with particular strength for headaches and nervous conditions centered in the head.
habitat: Open woodlands, meadows, and grassy heathlands across Europe
energetic_quality: Warm and dry, grounding and centering with a downward-directing energy
doctrine_of_signatures: The dense spike of flowers atop the stem resembles the spinal column and head, signaling its traditional use for headaches and its capacity to draw energy downward from an overactive mind.
safety_notes: Avoid during pregnancy due to uterine stimulant properties. Otherwise considered very safe. Large doses may cause nausea.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Nervine, cephalic, digestive tonic, grounding
parts.notes: Aerial parts gathered during flowering for optimal potency

parts.therapeutic_properties: Nervine, antispasmodic, mildly sedative
parts.notes: Flowers add additional calming properties to preparations

planet_associations.notes: Culpeper assigned wood betony to Jupiter and Aries, reflecting its beneficent healing power and its special affinity for the head.

zodiac_associations.notes: Aries rules the head, the primary site of wood betony's therapeutic action, addressing headaches, mental fog, and nervous tension in the cranium.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: One of the foremost herbal remedies for tension headaches and migraines, especially those arising from nervous tension
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2 teaspoons dried herb steeped 10-15 minutes, up to 3 cups daily

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Grounding nervine that draws scattered mental energy downward, relieving overthinking and worry
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2 mL tincture three times daily

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Bitter tonic properties support digestive function, particularly when poor digestion accompanies nervous tension
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1 cup infusion before meals

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Traditionally used to clear mental fog and restore focused awareness
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2 mL tincture in the morning and midday

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Grounds scattered, agitated energy and reduces tension headaches that accompany chronic irritability.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2 teaspoons dried herb per cup, steeped 10-15 minutes. Drink 2-3 cups daily.

contraindication_associations.reason: Wood betony (Stachys officinalis) is a recognized uterine stimulant that may induce contractions and has historical use as an emmenagogue and abortifacient
contraindication_associations.notes: Strictly avoid during pregnancy; traditionally contraindicated in all stages of gestation

contraindication_associations.reason: Wood betony may lower blood pressure through vasodilatory effects, potentially causing hypotension when combined with antihypertensive medications
contraindication_associations.notes: Monitor blood pressure if combining with antihypertensive drugs; may require medication dose adjustment

contraindication_associations.reason: Tannin content in wood betony may aggravate existing gastric or duodenal ulceration through astringent irritation of compromised mucosal tissue
contraindication_associations.notes: Use with caution in active peptic ulcer disease; consider alternative nervine herbs

description: Vervain is a slender, wiry perennial that has held sacred status across European cultures from the Druids to the Romans, who called it herba sacra. It acts as a nervine, digestive bitter, and liver tonic, with a particular gift for releasing tension held in the body by those who drive themselves too hard.
habitat: Roadsides, waste ground, dry grasslands, and sunny banks across Europe and temperate Asia
energetic_quality: Cool and dry, releasing and relaxing with a strongly descending energy
doctrine_of_signatures: The thin, wiry stems and tiny flowers suggest a plant for those who are themselves tense, wiry, and tightly wound, unable to relax and let go.
safety_notes: Avoid during pregnancy due to uterine stimulant action. May interfere with iron absorption. Not to be confused with lemon verbena (Aloysia citrodora).

parts.therapeutic_properties: Nervine, bitter tonic, hepatic, antispasmodic
parts.notes: Aerial parts harvested during flowering provide the full spectrum of medicinal activity

parts.therapeutic_properties: Nervine, relaxant, emotional release
parts.notes: Flowers are particularly valued in flower essence work for the vervain personality type

planet_associations.notes: Venus rules vervain through its association with love, sacred ritual, and the softening of hardened emotional states.

zodiac_associations.notes: Taurus, ruled by Venus, relates to vervain's grounding quality and its ability to release tension held in the neck, jaw, and shoulders.
Gemini's nervous, mercurial energy connects to the overactive mental state that vervain is best suited to calm.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Specifically indicated for stress from overwork and overcommitment, helping perfectionist types release and rest
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2 mL tincture three times daily

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Bitter hepatic that promotes bile flow and supports liver function under stress
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1 cup infusion before meals, 2-3 times daily

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Nervine and mood-supportive properties help lift depression associated with exhaustion and burnout
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 2 mL tincture three times daily

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Tension headaches and migraines respond well, particularly in those who carry stress in the neck and head
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2 cups infusion at onset

contraindication_associations.reason: Vervain (Verbena officinalis) has documented uterotonic activity and has been traditionally used as an emmenagogue; its verbenalin content may stimulate uterine smooth muscle contraction
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid all internal use during pregnancy; historically noted as a potential abortifacient at higher doses

contraindication_associations.reason: Vervain has complex neurological activity and may interact with anticonvulsant medications; while it has traditional anticonvulsant use, it may unpredictably alter seizure thresholds
contraindication_associations.notes: Use only under professional supervision in individuals with seizure disorders; do not substitute for prescribed anticonvulsant therapy

contraindication_associations.reason: Vervain has immunomodulatory properties that may stimulate immune activity and potentially exacerbate autoimmune conditions such as lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, or multiple sclerosis
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid in individuals with active autoimmune disease or those taking immunosuppressant medications

contraindication_associations.reason: Vervain may affect cardiac function through its verbenalin content, which can influence heart rate and rhythm, potentially interacting with cardiac medications
contraindication_associations.notes: Use with caution in individuals taking cardiac glycosides or antiarrhythmic medications

description: Elecampane is a tall, striking perennial with large sunflower-like blooms and a thick, aromatic root, named for Helen of Troy in classical legend. It is one of the great respiratory herbs of Western tradition, combining warming aromatics with immune-stimulating and expectorant properties.
habitat: Damp meadows, field edges, roadsides, and riverbanks across Europe and western Asia
energetic_quality: Warm and dry, aromatic and stimulating with a deep penetrating quality
doctrine_of_signatures: The large, sunny flower head and the deep, pungent root suggest a plant that brings solar warmth deep into the lungs to dry dampness and dispel cold, stagnant phlegm.
safety_notes: May cause allergic reactions in individuals sensitive to Asteraceae family plants. Avoid during pregnancy. Large doses may cause nausea and diarrhea.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Expectorant, antimicrobial, warming respiratory tonic, prebiotic
parts.notes: Root harvested in the second or third autumn contains the highest concentration of volatile oils and inulin

parts.therapeutic_properties: Mild expectorant, uplifting
parts.notes: Flowers have milder respiratory effects and are sometimes added to root preparations

planet_associations.notes: Mercury governs the lungs, respiration, and communication, all central to elecampane's therapeutic domain.
The Sun's warming, vitalizing energy corresponds to elecampane's capacity to bring warmth and vitality to cold, damp respiratory conditions.

zodiac_associations.notes: Gemini, ruled by Mercury, governs the lungs and airways, the primary therapeutic target of elecampane.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Powerful warming expectorant for chronic bronchitis, persistent cough, and cold, damp lung conditions
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2 teaspoons dried root simmered 20 minutes, 2-3 cups daily

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Antimicrobial volatile oils combined with immune stimulation help resolve deep-seated respiratory infections
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 2-3 mL tincture three times daily

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Inulin-rich root supports gut flora while bitter and aromatic compounds stimulate digestion
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2 mL tincture before meals

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Traditional vermifuge, with volatile oils showing activity against intestinal parasites
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Strong decoction taken on an empty stomach under practitioner guidance

contraindication_associations.reason: Elecampane (Inula helenium) has emmenagogue properties and contains alantolactone, a sesquiterpene lactone that may stimulate uterine contractions
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid during pregnancy; traditionally used to promote menstruation, indicating uterine-stimulating potential

contraindication_associations.reason: Elecampane belongs to the Asteraceae family and contains alantolactone, a potent contact allergen and sensitizer that can cause severe allergic dermatitis
contraindication_associations.notes: Contraindicated in individuals with Asteraceae family allergies; alantolactone is a recognized strong sensitizer in dermatological testing

contraindication_associations.reason: Elecampane root contains high concentrations of inulin, which may affect blood sugar regulation and potentiate hypoglycemic medications
contraindication_associations.notes: Monitor blood glucose levels when using alongside diabetes medications

contraindication_associations.reason: Sesquiterpene lactones in elecampane, particularly alantolactone, may irritate the gastric mucosa and worsen existing peptic ulceration
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid in active peptic ulcer disease; use lower doses and take with food if gastrointestinal sensitivity is present

description: Angelica is a majestic biennial or short-lived perennial of northern Europe, steeped in lore as a plant of angelic protection revealed during times of plague. Its warming, aromatic root and seeds serve as powerful circulatory stimulants and digestive tonics.
habitat: Damp meadows, stream banks, and cool mountain regions of northern and central Europe, Scandinavia, and Iceland
energetic_quality: Warm and dry, aromatic and strongly circulating with an uplifting quality
doctrine_of_signatures: The hollow, channeled stems and large spherical flower heads radiating outward suggest the plant's ability to open channels, move stagnant energy, and circulate warmth to the periphery of the body.
safety_notes: Contains furanocoumarins that may cause photosensitivity. Avoid excessive sun exposure during use. Avoid during pregnancy. Ensure correct identification as the Apiaceae family contains deadly lookalikes.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Circulatory stimulant, carminative, bitter tonic, expectorant, warming
parts.notes: Root harvested in the first autumn provides the richest aromatic oil content

parts.therapeutic_properties: Carminative, digestive stimulant, warming
parts.notes: Seeds are milder than root but share similar warming digestive properties

planet_associations.notes: The Sun's warming, protective, and vitalizing energy is central to angelica's reputation as a plant of divine protection and radiant warmth.

zodiac_associations.notes: Leo's solar rulership and association with the heart and vital warmth aligns with angelica's powerful circulatory and warming properties.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Strong circulatory stimulant that brings warmth to the extremities and improves peripheral blood flow
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2 mL tincture three times daily

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Aromatic bitter that stimulates appetite, bile flow, and relieves bloating and flatulence
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1 mL tincture before meals

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Warming expectorant that helps clear cold, damp congestion from the lungs
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1 teaspoon dried root simmered 15 minutes, 2 cups daily

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: General tonic and warming stimulant that restores vitality, especially in cold and depleted constitutions
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 2 mL tincture twice daily

contraindication_associations.reason: Angelica (Angelica archangelica) is a potent uterine stimulant with documented emmenagogue and abortifacient properties; its furanocoumarins and essential oil compounds directly stimulate uterine smooth muscle
contraindication_associations.notes: Strictly contraindicated during pregnancy; angelica has been historically used to induce menstruation and labor

contraindication_associations.reason: Angelica contains coumarins and furanocoumarins (angelicin, bergapten) that have anticoagulant activity by inhibiting vitamin K-dependent clotting factor synthesis
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid concurrent use with warfarin, heparin, or antiplatelet drugs; discontinue at least two weeks before surgery

contraindication_associations.reason: Angelica has demonstrated hypoglycemic effects and may potentiate insulin and oral hypoglycemic agents, increasing the risk of dangerous hypoglycemia
contraindication_associations.notes: Monitor blood glucose closely when using angelica alongside diabetes medications

contraindication_associations.reason: Furanocoumarins in angelica (bergapten, xanthotoxin) cause photosensitization, leading to phytophotodermatitis with severe skin burns upon UV exposure
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid sun exposure after topical application or high-dose internal use; particularly dangerous in fair-skinned individuals

description: Wild cherry is a deciduous tree native to eastern North America whose bark has been used for centuries as a cough suppressant and respiratory sedative. The inner bark contains cyanogenic glycosides that, in small doses, calm irritated cough reflexes and soothe spasmodic respiratory conditions.
habitat: Mixed deciduous and mixed forests, forest edges, and old fields across eastern North America
energetic_quality: Cool and dry, sedating and astringent with a gentle calming quality
doctrine_of_signatures: The reddish-brown inner bark suggests an affinity for the bronchial passages, and the tree's capacity to grow quietly in the understory reflects the gentle, quieting nature of its cough-suppressing action.
safety_notes: Contains cyanogenic glycosides; use only properly prepared bark in recommended doses. Avoid wilted leaves which concentrate toxic compounds. Not for long-term use. Avoid during pregnancy.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Antitussive, sedative, astringent, mild expectorant
parts.notes: Inner bark harvested in autumn; cold preparations preserve the volatile cyanogenic compounds best

parts.therapeutic_properties: Antioxidant, mild astringent, nutritive
parts.notes: Ripe fruits are edible and mildly astringent; used in syrups and preserves

planet_associations.notes: Cherry trees are traditionally associated with Venus for their beauty, their sweet fruit, and their gentle, soothing medicinal action.
The Moon's calming, sedating influence connects to wild cherry bark's ability to quiet irritated coughs and calm restless breathing.

zodiac_associations.notes: Taurus rules the throat and is governed by Venus, connecting to wild cherry's primary action on the throat and upper respiratory passages.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: One of the best herbal cough suppressants, particularly for dry, irritating, spasmodic coughs
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Cold infusion or syrup: 1-2 teaspoons bark steeped in cold water 4-8 hours, 2-3 cups daily

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Mild sedative properties help when coughing disrupts sleep
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1 cup cold infusion before bed

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Bitter and astringent properties gently tone a lax digestive tract
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2 mL tincture before meals

contraindication_associations.reason: Wild cherry bark contains prunasin, a cyanogenic glycoside that is hydrolyzed to hydrogen cyanide (HCN); cyanide crosses the placental barrier and is toxic to fetal development
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid during pregnancy; even small amounts of cyanogenic glycosides pose a teratogenic and fetotoxic risk

contraindication_associations.reason: Cyanogenic glycosides and their hydrolysis products may pass into breast milk, posing a toxicity risk to nursing infants whose detoxification capacity is immature
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid wild cherry bark preparations during breastfeeding

contraindication_associations.reason: Cyanide detoxification occurs primarily in the liver via the rhodanese enzyme pathway; individuals with hepatic impairment have reduced capacity to convert cyanide to thiocyanate, increasing toxicity risk
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid in significant liver disease; use only small, controlled doses under professional guidance if liver function is adequate

contraindication_associations.reason: Thiocyanate, the primary detoxification product of cyanogenic glycosides, is renally excreted; impaired kidney function may lead to thiocyanate accumulation and toxicity
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid in individuals with significant renal impairment; monitor renal function if use is deemed necessary

description: Rue is a strongly aromatic, blue-green evergreen shrub native to the Mediterranean, once considered one of the most potent protective herbs in European magic and medicine. Its intensely bitter and pungent character reflects its powerful but potentially harsh medicinal action on the nervous system and uterus.
habitat: Rocky, dry limestone soils, garden walls, and sunny slopes around the Mediterranean basin
energetic_quality: Warm and dry, strongly stimulating and dispersing with an acrid, bitter taste
doctrine_of_signatures: The blue-green, translucent, gland-dotted leaves when held to the light reveal their potent volatile oil content, suggesting a plant of penetrating vision and the capacity to see through darkness, aligning with its traditional use for eye health.
safety_notes: Strongly contraindicated in pregnancy as a potent emmenagogue and abortifacient. Can cause severe photosensitivity and contact dermatitis. Use only in small, carefully measured doses under practitioner guidance. Toxic in excess.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Antispasmodic, emmenagogue, nervine, bitter tonic, circulatory stimulant
parts.notes: Fresh leaves are most potent but also most irritating; dried herb is safer for internal preparations

parts.therapeutic_properties: Antispasmodic, mild circulatory stimulant
parts.notes: Young stems are used along with leaves in aerial herb preparations

planet_associations.notes: Mars rules rue through its acrid, hot, penetrating, and aggressive medicinal character and its association with protection and warding.
The Sun connects to rue's traditional use for sharpening eyesight and its reputation as an herb of clarity and illumination.

zodiac_associations.notes: Aries' martial rulership and headstrong nature mirrors rue's forceful, penetrating action and its historical use as an herb of bold protection.
Scorpio's connection to the reproductive system and transformation aligns with rue's powerful emmenagogue properties and its historical role in rites of passage.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Rutin strengthens capillaries in the eye, with a long traditional reputation for improving and preserving vision
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Very small doses only: pinch of dried herb in 1 cup water, 1 cup daily maximum

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Potent emmenagogue that promotes delayed menstruation; must be used with extreme caution
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 0.5-1 mL tincture under practitioner supervision only

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Rutin and volatile oils improve peripheral circulation and strengthen capillary walls
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Small infusion doses, practitioner-guided

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Antispasmodic and nervine properties address neuralgic pain, particularly in the face and head
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 0.5-1 mL tincture up to twice daily

contraindication_associations.reason: Rue (Ruta graveolens) is one of the most potent herbal abortifacients known; it contains pilocarpine, rutin, and furanocoumarins that cause violent uterine contractions, and its essential oil is directly embryotoxic and teratogenic
contraindication_associations.notes: Absolutely contraindicated during pregnancy at any dose; rue has caused maternal death from hemorrhage following its use as an abortifacient

contraindication_associations.reason: Rue contains hepatotoxic furanocoumarins and alkaloids (arborinine, dictamnine) that are metabolized to reactive intermediates capable of causing hepatocellular damage
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid in individuals with liver disease; even small doses may cause hepatotoxic effects in compromised liver function

contraindication_associations.reason: Rue is nephrotoxic at moderate to high doses; its toxic alkaloids and volatile oils can cause acute kidney injury and renal tubular necrosis
contraindication_associations.notes: Strictly avoid in individuals with renal impairment; cases of acute kidney injury have been reported

contraindication_associations.reason: Rue contains potent photosensitizing furanocoumarins (psoralen, bergapten, xanthotoxin) that cause severe phytophotodermatitis with blistering and skin necrosis upon UV exposure
contraindication_associations.notes: Handle the plant with gloves; avoid all sun exposure after any contact with rue; can cause permanent scarring

contraindication_associations.reason: Rue's volatile oils and alkaloids are potent gastrointestinal irritants that can cause mucosal erosion, gastritis, and worsen existing peptic ulceration
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid in individuals with active peptic ulcer disease or inflammatory bowel conditions

description: Ginseng is one of the most revered adaptogenic herbs in traditional Chinese medicine, prized for its ability to restore vital energy and strengthen the body's resistance to stress. The fleshy, forked root has been used for millennia as a supreme tonic for longevity, mental clarity, and overall vitality.
habitat: Shaded, cool mountainous forests with rich, well-drained soil at elevations between 200 and 1000 meters.
energetic_quality: Warm, sweet, slightly bitter; tonifies Qi and Yang, nourishes Yin of the lung and spleen.
doctrine_of_signatures: The humanoid shape of the root, often resembling a full human figure with arms and legs, signifies its capacity to treat the whole body and restore total vitality.
safety_notes: Avoid with hypertension or acute infections. May interact with blood thinners and MAO inhibitors. Not recommended during pregnancy. Overuse may cause overstimulation, insomnia, or headaches.

parts.typical_compounds: Ginsenosides (Rb1, Rg1, Rg3), polysaccharides, polyacetylenes, peptidoglycans
parts.therapeutic_properties: Adaptogenic, immunomodulatory, anti-fatigue, nootropic, hypoglycemic
parts.notes: The primary medicinal part; aged or steamed red ginseng is considered more potent than fresh white ginseng.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Mild adaptogenic, antioxidant, anti-inflammatory
parts.notes: Leaves contain similar but lower concentrations of active compounds and are sometimes used in teas.

planet_associations.notes: Ginseng embodies solar vitality, life force, and the restoration of core energy, mirroring the Sun's role as the source of all life.
Its stimulating, warming nature and ability to increase physical stamina align with Martian strength and drive.

zodiac_associations.notes: Ginseng's sovereign tonic status and solar rulership align with Leo's regal vitality and heart-centered life force.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Ginsenosides enhance mitochondrial function and regulate the HPA axis, reducing both physical and mental fatigue.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2 grams dried root simmered for 30-45 minutes, taken 1-2 times daily.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Polysaccharides and ginsenosides modulate immune cell activity, increasing natural killer cell function and T-cell response.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 2-4 mL of 1:5 tincture taken 2-3 times daily for immune support cycles of 8-12 weeks.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Ginsenosides improve insulin sensitivity and promote glucose uptake in peripheral tissues.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 200-400 mg standardized extract taken before meals.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Red ginseng in particular has demonstrated improvement in erectile function and sexual arousal through nitric oxide pathway enhancement.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-3 grams of red ginseng root decocted daily for a minimum of 8 weeks.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Enhances energy, reduces fatigue, and supports immune function through adaptogenic mechanisms.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 200-400 mg standardized extract daily, taken in the morning.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Restores energy and supports adrenal gland function through adaptogenic modulation of the HPA axis.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 200-400 mg standardized extract daily.

contraindication_associations.reason: Ginseng's immunostimulatory ginsenosides upregulate T-cell proliferation and cytokine production, potentially exacerbating autoimmune flares by amplifying aberrant immune responses against self-antigens.
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid in lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, multiple sclerosis, and other autoimmune disorders. Discontinue if symptoms worsen.

contraindication_associations.reason: Ginsenosides exhibit estrogenic activity by binding estrogen receptor-beta and modulating aromatase expression, which may stimulate proliferation of estrogen-receptor-positive tumor cells.
contraindication_associations.notes: Contraindicated in estrogen-receptor-positive breast, ovarian, and uterine cancers. Discuss with oncologist before use.

contraindication_associations.reason: Ginsenosides inhibit platelet aggregation via suppression of thromboxane A2 formation and may potentiate the effects of anticoagulant medications such as warfarin.
contraindication_associations.notes: Discontinue at least 7 days before surgery. Monitor INR closely if taking concurrent anticoagulants.

contraindication_associations.reason: Ginseng enhances insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells and increases peripheral glucose uptake, which may cause hypoglycemia when combined with antidiabetic medications.
contraindication_associations.notes: Monitor blood glucose frequently. Dose adjustments of diabetes medications may be necessary.

contraindication_associations.reason: Ginsenoside Rb1 has demonstrated teratogenic effects in animal embryo studies, and the herb's hormonal activity poses risks to fetal development.
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid throughout pregnancy. Insufficient safety data in human pregnancies.

description: Ginkgo biloba is the sole surviving member of an ancient lineage of trees dating back over 270 million years, making it one of the oldest living tree species on Earth. Its fan-shaped leaves have been used in traditional Chinese medicine for centuries to enhance memory, improve circulation, and support respiratory health.
habitat: Temperate forests and cultivated landscapes; remarkably hardy and tolerant of pollution, making it common in urban plantings worldwide.
energetic_quality: Neutral to cool, bitter and astringent; clears heat from the lungs and moves blood stagnation.
doctrine_of_signatures: The bilobed leaf, resembling the two hemispheres of the brain, signals its affinity for enhancing cognitive function, memory, and cerebral blood flow.
safety_notes: May increase bleeding risk; avoid with anticoagulant medications. Raw seeds are toxic and should not be consumed. Allergic skin reactions possible from contact with the fruit pulp.

parts.typical_compounds: Ginkgolides A, B, C, and J, bilobalide, quercetin, kaempferol, isorhamnetin, proanthocyanidins
parts.therapeutic_properties: Vasodilatory, neuroprotective, antioxidant, platelet-activating factor inhibitor, cognitive enhancer
parts.notes: Standardized leaf extracts (EGb 761) are the most researched form, typically containing 24% flavone glycosides and 6% terpene lactones.

parts.typical_compounds: Ginkgolic acids, bilobol, starch, protein, lipids
parts.therapeutic_properties: Astringent, antitussive, used traditionally for urinary issues and wheezing
parts.notes: Seeds (bai guo) are used in Chinese medicine after careful processing to reduce toxicity; typically limited to 5-10 cooked seeds per serving.

planet_associations.notes: Ginkgo's primary association with mental acuity, communication between neurons, and cognitive speed aligns with Mercury's dominion over the intellect and nervous system.
As an ancient survivor bridging deep geological time, ginkgo resonates with Uranus's connection to evolutionary leaps and unconventional resilience.

zodiac_associations.notes: The bilobed leaf and Mercury rulership connect ginkgo to Gemini's domain of mental duality, communication, and neural connectivity.
Ginkgo's ancient lineage and unique evolutionary position resonate with Aquarian themes of standing apart and bridging past wisdom to future innovation.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Ginkgolides and bilobalide improve cerebral microcirculation and protect neurons from oxidative damage, supporting mental clarity and focus.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 120-240 mg standardized extract daily in divided doses, or 2-4 mL tincture twice daily.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Inhibition of platelet-activating factor and vasodilation improve peripheral and cerebral blood flow.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 120-160 mg standardized extract daily in 2-3 divided doses.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Improved microcirculation to the vestibular system and inner ear reduces vertigo symptoms.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 160-240 mg standardized extract daily for at least 8-12 weeks.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Enhanced retinal blood flow and antioxidant protection support glaucoma management and age-related visual decline.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 120 mg standardized extract daily.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Improves cerebral blood flow and provides antioxidant neuroprotection, supporting cognitive function often impaired in MS.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 120-240 mg standardized extract daily in divided doses.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Improves peripheral circulation and microvascular blood flow, reducing the frequency and severity of vasospastic episodes.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 120-240 mg standardized extract daily in divided doses.

contraindication_associations.reason: Ginkgolides, particularly ginkgolide B, are potent platelet-activating factor (PAF) antagonists that inhibit platelet aggregation and prolong bleeding time significantly.
contraindication_associations.notes: Contraindicated with anticoagulants (warfarin, heparin) and antiplatelet drugs. Stop use at least 14 days before surgical procedures.

contraindication_associations.reason: Ginkgo toxin (4'-O-methylpyridoxine) present in leaf preparations depletes GABA levels by inhibiting glutamic acid decarboxylase, lowering the seizure threshold.
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid in epilepsy and other seizure disorders. May reduce effectiveness of anticonvulsant medications.

contraindication_associations.reason: The antiplatelet activity of ginkgolides increases maternal hemorrhage risk, and ginkgolic acids may have cytotoxic effects on developing fetal tissue.
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid throughout pregnancy due to bleeding risk during delivery and potential fetal toxicity.

contraindication_associations.reason: Ginkgolic acids are structurally related to urushiol (the allergen in poison ivy) and can trigger contact dermatitis and cross-reactive allergic responses in sensitized individuals.
contraindication_associations.notes: Individuals with known allergy to Anacardiaceae family (mango, cashew, poison ivy) are at higher risk. Discontinue at first sign of rash.

description: Turmeric is a golden-yellow rhizomatous herb that has been a cornerstone of Ayurvedic and traditional Chinese medicine for thousands of years. Its vibrant color and potent anti-inflammatory properties have made it one of the most extensively studied medicinal plants in modern pharmacology.
habitat: Tropical and subtropical regions with warm temperatures, high humidity, and well-drained loamy soils rich in organic matter.
energetic_quality: Warm, bitter, pungent; moves blood, resolves stasis, clears damp-heat, and kindles digestive fire (Agni).
doctrine_of_signatures: The deep golden-yellow color of turmeric signals its affinity for the liver and bile production, while its warming nature connects it to digestive fire and the solar plexus.
safety_notes: High doses may cause gastrointestinal upset. Avoid therapeutic doses with gallstones or bile duct obstruction. May interact with blood thinners and diabetes medications. Bioavailability is greatly enhanced when paired with black pepper (piperine).

parts.therapeutic_properties: Anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, hepatoprotective, cholagogue, analgesic, antimicrobial
parts.notes: The rhizome is the primary medicinal part; curcumin constitutes approximately 2-5% of the dried rhizome by weight.

parts.typical_compounds: Essential oils (turmerones, ar-turmerone), flavonoids, phenolic compounds
parts.therapeutic_properties: Antioxidant, mild antimicrobial, digestive support
parts.notes: Leaves are used in traditional cooking as wraps and contribute aromatic essential oils, though they are less medicinally potent than the rhizome.

planet_associations.notes: Turmeric's golden color, liver affinity, expansive healing properties, and sacred status in Hindu tradition connect it to Jupiter's themes of abundance, wisdom, and benevolence.
Its radiant golden hue and warming, vitalizing properties mirror solar energy and life-giving force.

zodiac_associations.notes: Under Jupiter's rulership, turmeric's association with philosophical traditions, sacred ceremony, and liver support connects it to Sagittarius's expansive, truth-seeking nature.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Curcumin inhibits NF-kB, COX-2, and multiple inflammatory cytokines, rivaling some pharmaceutical anti-inflammatories in clinical trials.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 500-1000 mg curcumin extract with piperine, taken 2-3 times daily with food.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Reduces synovial inflammation and cartilage degradation markers in osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2 teaspoons powdered root simmered in milk or water with black pepper, taken twice daily.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Stimulates bile production and flow, supports hepatocyte regeneration, and enhances phase II liver detoxification.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 2-5 mL of 1:3 tincture taken 2-3 times daily before meals.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Promotes gastric motility, reduces bloating, and increases bile secretion for improved fat digestion.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1 teaspoon powdered turmeric steeped in hot water with ginger and honey, taken after meals.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Curcumin modulates inflammatory pathways implicated in MS, reducing neuroinflammation and oxidative damage to myelin sheaths.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 500-1000 mg curcumin with piperine, 2-3 times daily with food.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Anti-inflammatory action protects blood vessel endothelium and improves vascular function, reducing vasospastic tendency.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 500-1000 mg curcumin with piperine, twice daily with food.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Anti-inflammatory action reduces ovarian inflammation and improves insulin sensitivity, both key factors in PCOS pathology.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 500 mg curcumin with piperine, twice daily with food.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Curcumin reduces intestinal inflammation and supports gut barrier repair through modulation of tight junction proteins.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 500-1000 mg curcumin with piperine, twice daily with food.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Curcumin inhibits endometrial cell proliferation and reduces inflammatory mediators driving endometrial growth outside the uterus.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 500-1000 mg curcumin with piperine, 2-3 times daily with food.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Curcumin inhibits inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6) central to RA pathology, with clinical trials showing reduced joint swelling and tenderness.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 500-1000 mg curcumin with piperine, 2-3 times daily with food.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Curcumin modulates autoimmune inflammatory cascades and reduces proteinuria in lupus nephritis, with clinical evidence supporting its use.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 500-1000 mg curcumin with piperine, 2-3 times daily with food.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Curcumin reduces thyroid inflammation and modulates the autoimmune response targeting the thyroid gland.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 500-1000 mg curcumin with piperine, twice daily with food.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Curcumin reduces the central sensitization and neuroinflammation driving widespread pain in fibromyalgia.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 500-1000 mg curcumin with piperine, 2-3 times daily with food.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Curcumin reduces intestinal inflammation through NF-kB inhibition, with clinical trials showing maintained remission in Crohn's.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 500-1000 mg curcumin with piperine, 2-3 times daily with food.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Curcumin maintains remission in ulcerative colitis as adjunct therapy, with multiple clinical trials demonstrating reduced relapse rates.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2 g curcumin with piperine daily, in addition to standard therapy.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Curcumin inhibits the inflammatory pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-17) driving both skin and joint manifestations of psoriatic arthritis.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 500-1000 mg curcumin with piperine, 2-3 times daily with food.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Anti-inflammatory action reduces the autoimmune inflammation destroying moisture-producing glands.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 500-1000 mg curcumin with piperine, twice daily with food.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Curcumin reduces the inflammatory processes damaging pancreatic beta cells and improves insulin sensitivity.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 500-1000 mg curcumin with piperine, twice daily with food.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Curcumin broadly modulates autoimmune pathways by inhibiting NF-kB, reducing pro-inflammatory cytokines, and supporting T-regulatory cell function.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 500-1000 mg curcumin with piperine, 2-3 times daily with food.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Curcumin reduces vascular inflammation, improves endothelial function, and protects against atherosclerosis.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 500-1000 mg curcumin with piperine, twice daily with food.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Curcumin reduces hepatic inflammation, fibrosis, and oxidative stress in chronic liver disease.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 500-1000 mg curcumin with piperine, twice daily with food.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Curcumin reduces renal inflammation and fibrosis, with emerging evidence for slowing CKD progression.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 500 mg curcumin with piperine, twice daily with food.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Curcumin inhibits cancer cell proliferation, induces apoptosis, and modulates estrogen receptor activity in hormone-sensitive cancers.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 500-1000 mg curcumin with piperine, 2-3 times daily with food. Use as adjunct under oncologist guidance.

contraindication_associations.reason: Curcumin inhibits platelet aggregation by blocking thromboxane A2 synthesis and suppressing calcium mobilization in platelets, increasing hemorrhage risk.
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid high-dose supplementation with anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy. Culinary amounts are generally safe.

contraindication_associations.reason: At high doses, curcumin stimulates gastric acid secretion and may irritate the gastric mucosa, potentially aggravating existing peptic or duodenal ulcers.
contraindication_associations.notes: Use low doses with food if needed. Avoid concentrated extracts during active ulcer flares.

contraindication_associations.reason: Turmeric contains significant oxalate content (up to 2% by weight), which can increase urinary oxalate excretion and contribute to calcium oxalate kidney stone formation.
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid high-dose supplements in patients with history of oxalate kidney stones or chronic kidney disease.

contraindication_associations.reason: Curcumin has demonstrated uterotonic effects in animal models, stimulating uterine contractions that may increase risk of preterm labor or miscarriage at supplemental doses.
contraindication_associations.notes: Supplemental doses should be avoided. Normal culinary use in cooking is generally considered safe.

description: Holy Basil, known as Tulsi in Ayurvedic tradition, is venerated as a sacred plant embodying the goddess Lakshmi and is grown in nearly every Hindu household for spiritual protection and medicinal use. It is classified as a Rasayana (rejuvenative) herb that promotes longevity, balances the doshas, and acts as a powerful adaptogen for body and mind.
habitat: Tropical and subtropical climates; thrives in warm, moist conditions with well-drained soil and full sunlight.
energetic_quality: Warm, pungent, slightly bitter; opens the heart and mind, clears kapha, balances vata, and kindles agni without aggravating pitta excessively.
doctrine_of_signatures: The aromatic intensity and purple-tinged varieties suggest its capacity to purify the subtle energy body and protect against negative influences, while the plant's robust, upright growth signals vitality and spiritual strength.
safety_notes: Generally very safe. May have mild blood-thinning effects. Some sources suggest caution during pregnancy due to potential uterine stimulation. May enhance the effects of hypoglycemic medications.

parts.typical_compounds: Eugenol, rosmarinic acid, apigenin, ursolic acid, linalool, beta-caryophyllene, ocimumosides
parts.therapeutic_properties: Adaptogenic, anxiolytic, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, immunomodulatory, hypoglycemic
parts.notes: The leaves are the most commonly used part, consumed fresh, dried, or as tea; three main cultivars exist: Rama (green), Krishna (purple), and Vana (wild).

parts.typical_compounds: Mucilage, fixed oils, ursolic acid, flavonoids
parts.therapeutic_properties: Demulcent, cooling, digestive, mild laxative
parts.notes: Seeds swell into a mucilaginous gel when soaked and are used in Ayurvedic beverages for cooling and digestive purposes.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Mild nervine, aromatic, expectorant
parts.notes: Flowers are used in teas and spiritual offerings; they carry a concentrated aromatic profile.

planet_associations.notes: Tulsi's sacred association with Lakshmi (goddess of love and abundance), its heart-opening properties, and its role in devotional practice connect it to Venus's domain of love, harmony, and beauty.
Its aromatic nature, nervine properties, and ability to sharpen mental clarity link it to Mercury's governance of the nervous system and intellect.

zodiac_associations.notes: Tulsi's Venusian rulership and its capacity to restore balance across all three doshas connect it to Libra's archetype of harmony, equilibrium, and relational grace.
Its meticulous purification properties and role as a household healing herb resonate with Virgo's devotion to health, service, and discernment.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Ocimumosides and rosmarinic acid modulate cortisol and corticosterone levels, reducing physiological and psychological stress markers.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2 teaspoons dried leaf steeped for 5-10 minutes, taken 2-3 times daily.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Ursolic acid and eugenol improve pancreatic beta-cell function and enhance insulin secretion and sensitivity.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 300-600 mg standardized extract daily, or 2-3 cups of tulsi tea.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Eugenol and other volatile oils act as bronchodilators and expectorants, easing cough, congestion, and bronchial spasm.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: Strong decoction of fresh leaves with ginger and honey, taken 2-3 times daily during acute respiratory episodes.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: GABAergic and serotonergic activity provides anxiolytic effects comparable to mild benzodiazepines without sedation.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 2-3 mL of 1:5 tincture taken 2-3 times daily.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Adaptogenic herb that reduces cortisol and blood glucose, addressing stress and insulin resistance components of PCOS.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2 teaspoons dried leaf per cup, steeped 10 minutes. Drink 2-3 cups daily.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Adaptogenic support for the HPA axis, reducing cortisol which can exacerbate thyroid autoimmunity.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2 teaspoons dried leaf per cup, steeped 10 minutes. Drink 2-3 cups daily.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Adaptogenic herb that reduces cortisol, improves energy, and supports the stress response system depleted in CFS.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2 teaspoons dried leaf per cup, steeped 10 minutes. Drink 2-3 cups daily.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Lowers elevated cortisol and supports adrenal function through adaptogenic modulation of the stress response.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2 teaspoons dried leaf per cup, steeped 10 minutes. Drink 2-3 cups daily.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Reduces fasting and postprandial blood glucose through multiple mechanisms including enhanced insulin secretion.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 1-2 teaspoons dried leaf per cup, steeped 10 minutes. Drink 2-3 cups daily.

contraindication_associations.reason: Ursolic acid and eugenol in holy basil exhibit anti-fertility and uterotonic properties, potentially interfering with implantation and promoting uterine contractions in animal studies.
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid during pregnancy and when trying to conceive. Known traditional use as an anti-fertility herb.

contraindication_associations.reason: Eugenol in holy basil inhibits platelet aggregation and thromboxane synthesis, which may prolong bleeding time and potentiate anticoagulant drugs.
contraindication_associations.notes: Discontinue at least 2 weeks before surgery. Use caution with concurrent anticoagulant therapy.

contraindication_associations.reason: Holy basil significantly lowers fasting and postprandial blood glucose by enhancing insulin secretion and improving insulin sensitivity, risking hypoglycemia with concurrent diabetes medications.
contraindication_associations.notes: Monitor blood glucose closely. Adjust antidiabetic medication dosage under medical supervision.

contraindication_associations.reason: The anti-fertility compounds in holy basil, including ursolic acid, may be excreted in breast milk and could affect infant hormonal development. Insufficient safety data exists.
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid supplemental doses during breastfeeding. Occasional culinary use is likely safe.

description: Astragalus, known as Huang Qi in traditional Chinese medicine, is one of the most important tonic herbs for fortifying Wei Qi (defensive energy) and supporting the spleen and lung organ systems. Its deep, fibrous yellow root has been used for over two thousand years to strengthen the body's resilience against disease and environmental stress.
habitat: Grasslands, open woodlands, and sandy or well-drained soils in temperate continental climates at elevations up to 2000 meters.
energetic_quality: Warm, sweet; tonifies Qi, raises Yang, strengthens the exterior, and promotes tissue regeneration.
doctrine_of_signatures: The long, fibrous, yellow root reaching deep into the earth suggests astragalus's ability to draw up deep reserves of vitality and strengthen the body's foundational energy from the ground up.
safety_notes: Avoid during acute infections or fever, as tonifying herbs may strengthen pathogens. Use caution with immunosuppressive drugs and autoimmune conditions. Generally very safe for long-term use.

parts.typical_compounds: Astragalosides (I-VII), cycloastragenol, astragalus polysaccharides (APS), flavonoids (formononetin, calycosin), saponins
parts.therapeutic_properties: Immunomodulatory, adaptogenic, cardioprotective, anti-aging (telomerase activation), hepatoprotective, diuretic
parts.notes: Roots are harvested from 4-7 year old plants for maximum potency; traditionally sliced and simmered in soups or decocted.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Mild immune support, antioxidant
parts.notes: Stems are occasionally used in traditional preparations but are considered significantly less potent than the root.

planet_associations.notes: Astragalus's expansive tonifying nature, its ability to increase vitality and protect against disease, and its generous, nourishing qualities align with Jupiter's themes of growth, protection, and abundance.

zodiac_associations.notes: Under Jupiter's rulership, astragalus's upward-lifting energy, optimistic vitality, and protective strength resonate with Sagittarius's expansive, adventurous spirit.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Astragalus polysaccharides increase macrophage activity, enhance T-cell proliferation, and stimulate interferon production.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 9-30 grams dried root simmered for 30-60 minutes, taken daily as a preventive tonic.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Tonifies spleen Qi deficiency patterns presenting as chronic fatigue, poor appetite, and loose stools.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 3-5 mL of 1:3 tincture taken 2-3 times daily.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Strengthens spleen function in transforming and transporting fluids, reducing edema associated with Qi deficiency.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 15-30 grams decocted with other diuretic herbs such as poria and atractylodes.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Prophylactic use strengthens Wei Qi (defensive energy) and reduces frequency and duration of upper respiratory infections.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 500-1000 mg standardized extract daily during cold and flu season as prevention.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Immune-modulating polysaccharides help rebalance immune function without overstimulation, supporting balanced immunity in autoimmune states.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 6-12 g dried root simmered for 20-30 minutes, taken daily. Use with caution during flares.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Immune tonic that addresses the persistent immune dysfunction and fatigue characteristic of CFS.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 6-12 g dried root simmered 20-30 minutes, taken daily.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Immune and energy tonic that supports adrenal recovery through deep nourishment of vital energy.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 6-12 g dried root simmered 20-30 minutes, taken daily.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Immune-modulating herb that helps restore immune balance rather than simply stimulating or suppressing. Use with caution during acute flares.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 6-12 g dried root simmered 20-30 minutes, taken daily during stable periods.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Clinical evidence supports astragalus for reducing proteinuria and improving kidney function in chronic kidney disease.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 6-12 g dried root simmered 20-30 minutes, taken daily.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Immune-enhancing polysaccharides support anti-cancer immunity and reduce side effects of conventional cancer treatments.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 6-12 g dried root simmered 20-30 minutes, taken daily.

contraindication_associations.reason: Astragalus polysaccharides potently stimulate macrophage activity, enhance natural killer cell cytotoxicity, and promote Th1 cytokine release, which can intensify autoimmune tissue destruction.
contraindication_associations.notes: Contraindicated in lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, and multiple sclerosis. Immune stimulation may trigger disease flares.

contraindication_associations.reason: Astragaloside IV has demonstrated uterotonic activity in animal models and the herb's potent immunomodulatory effects pose unpredictable risks during pregnancy.
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid during pregnancy due to insufficient human safety data and potential immune-mediated effects on the fetus.

contraindication_associations.reason: Astragalus polysaccharides enhance insulin sensitivity and stimulate pancreatic beta-cell function, potentially causing additive hypoglycemia with antidiabetic medications.
contraindication_associations.notes: Monitor blood glucose levels regularly. Coordinate with prescriber if using alongside diabetes medications.

description: Schisandra, known as Wu Wei Zi (five-flavor berry) in Chinese medicine, is unique among medicinal plants for containing all five fundamental tastes: sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and pungent. This remarkable berry is classified as a superior tonic that simultaneously nourishes all five yin organ systems and serves as one of the premier adaptogens for mental and physical endurance.
habitat: Mixed forests and forest margins in temperate regions, often growing as a climbing vine on trees and shrubs in moist, shaded environments.
energetic_quality: Warm, sour (predominant), with all five tastes present; astringes the lung Qi, nourishes kidney Yin, calms the spirit, and contains the essence.
doctrine_of_signatures: The five distinct flavors present in a single berry signify its unique ability to harmonize and nourish all five major organ systems simultaneously, suggesting a medicine of rare completeness.
safety_notes: May increase stomach acid; use caution with peptic ulcers or GERD. Avoid during acute infections with exterior patterns. May interact with medications metabolized by cytochrome P450 enzymes. Generally safe for long-term use.

parts.typical_compounds: Schisandrins (A, B, C), gomisin A and N, schisandrol A and B, lignans, organic acids (citric, malic), vitamins C and E
parts.therapeutic_properties: Adaptogenic, hepatoprotective, nootropic, astringent, antioxidant, anti-fatigue, nervine tonic
parts.notes: The dried berry is the primary medicinal part; both the flesh and seeds contain bioactive lignans, though the seeds hold the highest concentrations.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Mild adaptogenic, antioxidant
parts.notes: The woody vine stems are occasionally used in traditional formulas but are considered secondary to the berries.

planet_associations.notes: Schisandra's five-fold nature, its mental-enhancing properties, and its capacity to bridge multiple organ systems reflect Mercury's role as mediator, communicator, and integrator of diverse forces.
The berry's beauty, its capacity to nourish skin and preserve youthful vitality, and its kidney Yin-nourishing properties connect to Venus's domain of beauty and sensual regeneration.

zodiac_associations.notes: The five-flavor complexity and Mercury rulership connect schisandra to Gemini's multifaceted, versatile nature and love of variety.
Its liver-protective, purifying properties and precise harmonization of organ systems resonate with Virgo's themes of health, discrimination, and systemic order.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Schisandrins enhance mitochondrial antioxidant capacity and improve cellular energy production under physical and mental stress.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 3-6 grams dried berries simmered for 20-30 minutes, taken 1-2 times daily.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Schisandrin B and gomisin A protect hepatocytes from chemical damage and support liver regeneration and phase I/II detoxification.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 2-4 mL of 1:5 tincture taken 2-3 times daily.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Calms shen (spirit), nourishes heart Yin, and reduces night sweats, supporting peaceful sleep without sedation.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 3-6 grams decocted with other calming herbs such as suan zao ren (jujube seed), taken in the evening.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Lignan compounds modulate cortisol output and enhance adrenal reserve, improving stress tolerance and recovery.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 500-1000 mg standardized extract taken 1-2 times daily.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Adaptogenic berry that supports thyroid function and helps regulate the stress response that worsens autoimmune thyroiditis.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 2-4 ml tincture or 500 mg extract, twice daily.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Adaptogenic support for thyroid function, helping optimize hormonal output and supporting liver conversion of T4 to T3.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 2-4 ml tincture or 500 mg extract, twice daily.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Five-flavored adaptogen that reduces fatigue, enhances mental performance, and supports liver detoxification.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 2-4 ml tincture or 500 mg extract, twice daily.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Supports adrenal function and reduces stress-induced cortisol spikes while improving mental clarity and energy.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 2-4 ml tincture or 500 mg extract, twice daily.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Lignans protect hepatocytes from toxic damage and support liver regeneration in chronic liver conditions.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 2-4 ml tincture or 500 mg extract, twice daily.

contraindication_associations.reason: Schisandrin B has demonstrated uterine stimulant effects in animal studies, and the lignans in schisandra may disrupt hormonal balance critical to maintaining early pregnancy.
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid during pregnancy. Traditionally contraindicated in Chinese medicine during gestation.

contraindication_associations.reason: While schisandra is hepatoprotective at therapeutic doses, its lignans undergo extensive hepatic metabolism via CYP3A4 and CYP2C9, potentially increasing toxicity in patients with impaired liver function.
contraindication_associations.notes: Use with caution in liver disease. Reduced hepatic clearance may lead to accumulation. Monitor liver function tests.

contraindication_associations.reason: Schisandra stimulates gastric acid secretion via cholinergic pathways, which can aggravate peptic ulcer disease and delay mucosal healing.
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid during active ulcer episodes. Take with food if used cautiously in those with ulcer history.

contraindication_associations.reason: Schisandrin and related lignans modulate GABAergic neurotransmission and may interact unpredictably with anticonvulsant medications, potentially altering seizure thresholds.
contraindication_associations.notes: Use under neurologist supervision only. Monitor for changes in seizure frequency.

description: Rhodiola rosea, known as golden root or Arctic root, is a subarctic adaptogen with a long history of use in Tibetan, Chinese, and Scandinavian folk medicine for enhancing endurance, altitude tolerance, and resilience to extreme environments. Its rose-scented root contains potent compounds that regulate the stress response at the cellular level, making it one of the most clinically validated adaptogens in modern research.
habitat: High-altitude rocky slopes, sea cliffs, and arctic-alpine tundra, typically at elevations of 1000-5000 meters in cold, harsh climates.
native_region: Arctic and mountainous regions of Central Asia, Siberia, Scandinavia, and Tibet
energetic_quality: Cool to neutral, slightly bitter and astringent; clears stagnant heat from constraint, lifts the spirit, and consolidates Qi without overstimulation.
doctrine_of_signatures: Growing in the harshest arctic and alpine environments where few plants survive, rhodiola's resilience against extreme cold, wind, and altitude mirrors the resilience it imparts to those who use it, particularly under stress and adversity.
safety_notes: May cause initial stimulation or vivid dreams. Avoid combining with stimulants or in bipolar disorder during manic phases. Best taken in the morning or early afternoon to prevent sleep disruption. Generally well-tolerated.

parts.typical_compounds: Rosavins (rosavin, rosin, rosarin), salidroside, tyrosol, rhodioflavonoside, gallic acid
parts.therapeutic_properties: Adaptogenic, anti-fatigue, antidepressant, anxiolytic, neuroprotective, cardioprotective
parts.notes: Standardized extracts typically contain 3% rosavins and 1% salidroside; the root must be at least 5 years old for optimal potency and is harvested in autumn.

parts.typical_compounds: Phenolic acids, flavonoids, organic acids (lower bioactive concentration)
parts.therapeutic_properties: Mild antioxidant
parts.notes: Aerial parts are occasionally used in folk preparations but are significantly less potent than the root.

planet_associations.notes: Rhodiola's association with endurance through hardship, cold environments, high altitude, and perseverance under extreme conditions aligns with Saturn's themes of discipline, resilience, and mastery through adversity.
Its capacity to increase physical stamina, combat fatigue, and enhance performance under duress resonates with Mars's energy of strength and determined action.

zodiac_associations.notes: Rhodiola's Saturnian endurance, mountain habitat, and ability to function optimally under the most demanding conditions mirror Capricorn's relentless ascent and mastery of challenging terrain.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Salidroside and rosavins modulate serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine transport, improving mood with fewer side effects than SSRIs in mild to moderate depression.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 200-600 mg standardized extract daily, taken in the morning.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Reduces mental and physical fatigue by enhancing mitochondrial ATP production and modulating stress hormone output.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 2-3 mL of 1:5 tincture taken 1-2 times daily, morning and early afternoon.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Normalizes cortisol patterns and reduces stress-induced biomarkers including salivary cortisol and C-reactive protein.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 200-400 mg standardized extract taken in the morning on an empty stomach.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Enhances cognitive performance under stress and fatigue, improving attention, associative thinking, and short-term memory.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 2-3 mL tincture taken in the morning before demanding mental tasks.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Combats the profound fatigue and cognitive fog of fibromyalgia through adaptogenic HPA axis support.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 200-400 mg extract standardized to rosavins, taken in the morning.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Enhances cellular energy metabolism and reduces mental and physical fatigue through adaptogenic action on stress pathways.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 200-400 mg extract standardized to rosavins, taken in the morning.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Reduces cortisol output during stress and improves stress resilience, directly supporting adrenal recovery.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 200-400 mg extract standardized to rosavins, taken in the morning.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Adaptogenic support for stress-related psychiatric symptoms, with evidence for reducing fatigue, anxiety, and mild depression.
ailment_associations.dosage_notes: 200-400 mg extract standardized to rosavins daily.

contraindication_associations.reason: Rhodiola's active compounds salidroside and rosavin have not been adequately studied in pregnancy, and the herb's stimulatory effects on the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis pose theoretical risks to fetal development.
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid during pregnancy due to lack of safety data and potential hormonal disruption.

contraindication_associations.reason: Rhodiola rosea stimulates immune function by enhancing T-cell proliferation and natural killer cell activity, which may exacerbate autoimmune pathology.
contraindication_associations.notes: Use with caution in autoimmune conditions. Discontinue if disease activity increases.

contraindication_associations.reason: Rhodiola modulates serotonin and dopamine levels via inhibition of MAO-A and MAO-B enzymes, posing risk of serotonin syndrome when combined with SSRIs or MAOIs.
contraindication_associations.notes: Avoid combining with antidepressants, particularly SSRIs and MAOIs. May exacerbate mania in bipolar disorder.

contraindication_associations.reason: Rhodiola's stimulatory action on the sympathetic nervous system can elevate blood pressure and heart rate, particularly at higher doses.
contraindication_associations.notes: Monitor blood pressure when starting rhodiola. Use lower doses in those with borderline hypertension.

description: Licorice root, known as Gan Cao in Chinese medicine, is called the Great Harmonizer for its unparalleled ability to moderate and harmonize the effects of other herbs in compound formulas. It appears in more traditional Chinese medicine formulations than any other single herb, prized for its sweet flavor, its capacity to tonify Qi, moisten the lungs, and resolve toxicity.
habitat: Deep-soiled river valleys, semi-arid grasslands, and alluvial plains in temperate continental climates.
energetic_quality: Neutral to warm, sweet; tonifies spleen Qi, moistens lung, harmonizes all other herbs, and clears heat-toxins.
doctrine_of_signatures: The extraordinary sweetness of the root, fifty times sweeter than sugar, signals its capacity to harmonize and sweeten the effects of harsh or bitter herbs in a formula, acting as a peacemaker among disparate medicinal forces.
safety_notes: Prolonged use or high doses can cause pseudoaldosteronism with hypertension, hypokalemia, and edema. Avoid with hypertension, kidney disease, or hypokalemia. Limit use to 4-6 weeks without medical supervision. Deglycyrrhizinated licorice (DGL) is safer for long-term use.

parts.typical_compounds: Glycyrrhizin, glycyrrhetinic acid, liquiritigenin, isoliquiritigenin, glabridin, flavonoids, isoflavones
parts.therapeutic_properties: Demulcent, expectorant, anti-inflammatory, antiviral, adrenal supportive, harmonizing, antispasmodic
parts.notes: The root is typically harvested after 3-4 years of growth; both raw (sheng gan cao) and honey-fried (zhi gan cao) forms are used with different therapeutic emphases in Chinese medicine.

parts.therapeutic_properties: Mild demulcent, antioxidant
parts.notes: The stolons and runners are sometimes included in preparations alongside the taproot.

planet_associations.notes: Licorice's extreme sweetness, harmonizing nature, demulcent moistening quality, and ability to create peace among disparate herbal forces connect it to Venus's domain of harmony, sweetness, and relational grace.
Its generous, expansive healing nature and its role as the most frequently used herb in TCM formulas reflect Jupiter's benevolence and magnanimity.

zodiac_associations.notes: As the Great Harmonizer, licorice's primary function of balancing and mediating between other herbs is the botanical embodiment of Libra's quest for equilibrium and diplomatic accord.
Its Venusian sweetness, nourishing demulcent quality, and deep earthy root nature connect to Taurus's themes of sensory pleasure, sustenance, and grounded stability.

ailment_associations.efficacy_notes: Glycyrrhizin and liquiritigenin act as expectorants and demulcents, soothing irritated mucous membranes and loosening phlegm in both dry and productive coughs.
